responseSet.title: Modern Times
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Modern Times

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

The new year is one of exciting changes for JAPA. The American Psychoanalytic Association has partnered with our new publisher, Sage Publications, to bring JAPA subscribers the best in electronic publishing, while maintaining the high quality we have come to expect in the print edition. Online subscription is now available. Subscribers will eventually have access to all JAPA content back to our 1953 beginnings. The new online submission feature will make it easier for authors to submit and track their manuscripts, and will help streamline our Editorial Office operations. In addition, Sage's online publish-before-print option will allow papers to be published expeditiously, doing away with the sometimes lengthy wait for print publication. This new venture is of great benefit to JAPA authors, subscribers, and staff, as it brings speed, modernity, and connectivity to our slow-paced and often solitary work. Sitting in a comfortable armchair with our glossy, colorful print issue will now be complemented by access to cyberspace participation in the modern world of electronic scholarship. I know that there are pertinent concerns about speed and change. I am reminded of John Steinbeck on the then mushrooming interstate highway system. “You will shortly be able to get anywhere in the country without ever seeing anything!” We analysts will now have choices, with only ourselves to blame. It is really a life-cycle issue: “When are we going to be there? Where have we been?” JAPA readers will now have it all—a problem perhaps, but one best left for another day.

Comings and Goings

While we are very pleased with our new publishing arrangement, we are sad to say good-bye to Lawrence Schwartz, our former production editor. We thank him for his years of extraordinary dedication to JAPA—we will miss him greatly. We also thank Tamar Schwartz for all her help over the years.

We also say good-bye and thank you to another group of exceptional editorial readers who did prodigious amounts of work on JAPA

[[RunningHead]]

Violin Dreams. By Arnold Steinhardt. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 2006, 255 pp., $23.95.

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

400 Maynard Street, Suite 706
Ann Arbor, Ml 48104
E-mail: jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel  

This is an unusual book review submission to a psychoanalytic journal. Violin Dreams, by Arnold Steinhardt, first violinist of the Guarneri String Quartet and soloist in recitals with orchestra, is more than the liner notes indicate, “a love letter to the violin.” Violin Dreams reveals the author's natural analytic inclination to think deeply about himself and his music-making as integral to his inner life. His book is a contribution to psychoanalytic thinking outside the box of the consulting room and the formal concert hall. Over the course of its 255 pages, the reader is invited to eavesdrop on a process that feels as though he or she is privy to a series of quasi-analytic sessions. Steinhardt's previous book, Indivisible by Four (1996), chronicles the inception and history of the Guarneri Quartet, who retired after their 2008-2009 season and forty-five years of music-making.

[[RunningHead]]

False Self: The Life of Masud Khan. By Linda Hopkins. New York: Other Press, 2006, 551 pp., $35.00.

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

4701 Willard Avenue #204
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
E-mail: rwmd@comcast.net

Richard Waugaman  

For many years, Friedrich Nietzsche's reputation was badly tarnished by the false claims of Nazi propagandists that Nietzsche was a proto-Nazi.

309

Walter Kaufmann (1950) deserves major credit for rehabilitating Nietzsche's intellectual reputation. During the last decade of Nietzsche's life, his mental capacities deteriorated steadily, due probably to neurosyphilis. Masud Khan (1924-1989), a major contributor to the psychoanalytic literature, left such a legacy of outrageous behavior that his reputation may never recover. Like Nietzsche, his physical and mental health deteriorated severely in his final years. Many of us concluded that Khan was not worth reading after his final book (Khan 1988) revealed him to be a malignantly narcissistic bigot, and after his former patient, Wynne Godley (2001), opened the floodgates to many further horrifying revelations about Khan's professional and personal impairment.

If future clinicians read Khan, it will be in large measure thanks to Linda Hopkins's extraordinary book. She comes across as being as reliable a narrator as Khan was deceitful; as able to win the trust of the dozens of people she interviewed for this book as Khan was able to antagonize people; as scrupulously ethical as Khan was “psychopathic” (p. 381, quoting A. Green). Hopkins faced some ethical conundrums in deciding how to obtain and use crucial information, including material from several of Khan's former patients. She acknowledges that Glen Gabbard helped her resolve these quandaries (and he deserves to be listed in the index, since he is mentioned four times in the book). Hopkins quotes Khan's one-time friend André Green at the beginning of her book: “No one can deny Masud's talent. But it is also impossible to deny his sickness and his evil nature” (p. xix).

Khan appears to have been unanalyzable. Hopkins faults his three training analysts for their failure to set adequate limits on his egregious behavior. Khan became an analytic candidate in the first place through a misunderstanding, when Khan was only seeking a therapeutic analysis. Because of Khan's promise (and perhaps also because of his wealth), the first analyst he consulted persuaded the twenty-year-old Khan to seek instead a training analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis Past and Present

The Road to Unity in Psychoanalytic Theory. By Leo Rangell. Plymouth, UK: Jason Aronson, 2007, 132 pp., $34.95.

Richard Honig

Richard Honig

3001 Highland Avenue
Cincinnati, OH 45219
E-mail: rhonig@3001.us

Richard Honig  

To those familiar with the enormous body of work that is Leo Rangell's contribution to the psychoanalytic core, The Road to Unity in Psychoanalytic Theory will sound many a familiar chord. To those less well acquainted, this slim volume will provide a most accessible introduction to one of the major players on the psychoanalytic stage for well-nigh half its history. Beyond that, the book offers not only an analysis, but in fact a psychoanalysis, of the dynamic forces and family tensions that have shaped the theoretical discourse and divides since Freud attracted his earliest followers.

Rangell's dedication page for this treatise reads “The trunk of the psychoanalytic tree is the Freudian theory of the mind.” His intention is to sound an alarm that has reverberated through his writings for the past three decades concerning the ever advancing blight of theoretical pluralism infecting the once sturdy trunk. As he views matters, this now rampant pluralism has led the discipline of psychoanalysis to its present state of “disillusionment and demoralization. … conflict, confusion, contradictions and a fragmented whole” (p. 4). His current assessment seems a good deal more dire and his plea more desperate, lacking the optimism of his 1974 presidential address to the International Psychoanalytical Association, in which he stated that despite the storms of dissension, “over the rubble, psychoanalysis still stands, like a colossus, ravaged and gouged but always there, to the relief even of those who attack it” (p. 7).

In describing the disease infecting psychoanalysis and undermining its claim to scientific credibility, Rangell is also setting the stage for a reiteration of his solution to the crisis, which he first presented in his 1988 article “The Future of Psychoanalysis: The Scientific Crossroads.

319

[[RunningHead]]

Haunted by Parents. By Leonard Shengold. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2006, 257 pp., $35.00.

Joan Wexler

Joan Wexler

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: Joan.wexler@yale.edu

Joan Wexler  

In the 1930s and early 1940s Margaret Mead and Gregory Bateson made a film called Bathing Babies in Three Cultures. In the film they showed how physical handling and the attitude of the parent toward the child, in the ordinary act of bathing, contributes to the shaping of personality.

Like Mead and Bateson, Leonard Shengold addresses the question of how early body experiences, in the hands and attitude of our parents, shape our minds. Taking a highly sophisticated and thoughtful approach using work with adult analysands, Shengold pays close attention to his patients' childhood recollections of how their parents handled and responded to their growing bodies. He considers this in interplay with the child's developing body ego, current internalized parental imagoes, and the ever present drives. Along with very few others on the contemporary scene (usually those who write on the psychology of women), Shengold privileges the body and the body ego.

In his 1991 Father, Don't You See I'm Burning? Shengold lucidly described how our sexual and aggressive drives create an array of burning needs throughout our childhood and for the rest of our lives. Who we eventually become grows out of our endowments and deficiencies, but also out of the love and care or humiliation and abuse bestowed by our parents on our actual childhood flesh and spirit. How they regarded our physical and wishful selves—with indifference, scorn, abandonment, or overindulgence, or with a loving gaze and tender words—leaves its mark on our very habitus, as well as on our internalized self and other representations. Eventual neutralization of our driven desires (the ability to tolerate gratification that falls short of everything we want) depends on a benevolent and containing physical and emotional interaction with those who care for us.

With the ordinary, expectable conflicts that are a part of development, some body-ego wounding is inevitable. We soon create the character armor we need to protect ourselves. How supple or rigid our armor is, how truly protective or ominously self-destructive it is, will depend on what we internalize from our early experience of the physical and emotional care we receive. The rest of our development, compared to the complex dynamics of early parental handling and regard, is commentary.

[[RunningHead]]

Exploring the Invisible: Art Science, and the Spiritual. By Lynn Gamwell. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2002, 344 pp., $49.95 hardcover, $35.00 paperback.

Alexandra Harrison

Alexandra Harrison

183 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: aharrisonmd@comcast.net

Alexandra Harrison  

This beautiful and scholarly book by Lynn Gamwell—director of the Art Museum at the State University of New York, Binghamton, curator of the Gallery of Art and Science at the New York Academy of Sciences, and adjunct professor of Science at the School of Visual Arts, New York—sets the goal of “illustrating the influence of the scientific world view on the visual arts and on the development of abstract art.” The book is of potential interest to psychoanalysts both because Gamwell's list of scientific worldviews includes Freud's contributions and because the book considers how changes occur—in particular, how changes in science lead to changes in the visual arts—and the process of change is a central issue in psychoanalysis.

The book is organized in thirteen chapters that cover two centuries of art beginning with “Romanticism” and ending with “Postmodernism.” The text is well written and offers a fascinating survey of the explosion of scientific discovery in this period. In addition, the book is filled with splendid illustrations of the art. Exploring the Invisible is not, however, a casual read; a reader faces the challenge of learning about some complicated scientific advances and their links to contemporaneous artistic developments.

The two centuries explored in the book begin with the French Revolution, which Gamwell sets as the starting point for trends toward modern science and secularism. These trends provided support for a growing middle class that tended to embrace the egalitarian perspective of the scientific method, as well as the values of individualism and self-determination that underlie the field of psychology and the romantic movement. For the geographic center of the movement she first chooses Germany, particularly the philosophy of German Idealism with its vision of the unity of nature preparing the way for the science and art of the future. After the end of World War II, she relocates the geographic center to the United States, with its culture of heterogeneity and utilitarianism, and shifts the artistic focus to abstract art. In distinguishing between earlier versions of abstract art and the abstract art that emerged after the Second World War, Gamwell identifies a wave of abstraction precipitated by the revolution in physics that raised the issue about the extent to which the artist can visualize the invisible realms revealed by science.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narratives of Inner Life

The Center Cannot Hold. By Elyn R. Saks. New York: Hyperion, 2007, 340 pp., $24.95.

T. Downey

T. Downey

132 Sachems Head Road
Guilford, CT 06437
E-mail: twdowney@comcast.net

T. Downey  

In the prologue of The Center Cannot Hold, Elyn Saks immediately introduces the imagery of her disorder, madness, mayhem, apocalypse, and guilt. With her first question—“Have you ever killed anyone?”—she presents a murderous-mindedness in the midst of self/other confusion. In her feverish, psychotically commandeered mind, in which her superego runs amok, her two companions morph into “partners in crime.” The room/roof architecture dissolves, to be replaced by areas of safety and danger. She introduces her origins ominously as golden Florida lemons metaphorically morph into dark demons. There is paranoid talk of “casing the joint” as her friends ambiguously become linked in a psychotic mind crime. A piece of wire is found and secreted along with a nail. Are they for protection or assault, for self-defense or to inflict mayhem? Throughout her story this question hovers around her intents—an enduring psychotic confusion. By the time Elyn reaches the hospital, her internal collapse is complete, and the psychotic betrayal that set firestorms in her mind and consumed most of her rational self has been projected onto the hospital staff. The actions of the staff, rough handling and callous, insensitive interactions, play into her persecutory anxieties. Their maneuvers, ostensibly taken to protect her and them from unbridled instinctual rage, are more easily interpreted by her as abusive and demonic. This distrust of staff through many hospitalizations and psychotic iterations seems to be more than mere paranoia. A neurophysiological collapse in the functioning of the brain is almost inevitably read by what is left of a meaning-making mind as a catastrophic helplessness in the face of tyrannical external authorities, rather than internal chaos.

Elyn Saks takes the title of her memoir from Yeats's “The Second Coming” (1920), a song of fury about a loveless world of Christian decay.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and History

Freud in Zion: The History of Psychoanalysis in Jewish Palestine/Israel, 1918-1948 (Hebrew). By Eran J. Rolnik. Tel Aviv: Am Oved, 2007, 335 pp., 89 new shekels.

Zvi Lothane

Zvi Lothane

1435 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: schreber@lothane.com

Zvi Lothane  

In the wake of the defection of the three prominent dissidents Adler, Stekel, and Jung, the first major political crisis in psychoanalysis and a portent of many more to come, Freud (1914) published “On the History of the Psycho-Analytic Movement.” Movement or science? Freud claimed for psychoanalysis “the right to be valued as a science” (p. 59), vigorously polemicizing against hostile critics and horrible heretics. Freud also conceded that the young science he created was a “scientific movement [with] upheavals and dissensions” (p. 49). But upheavals and dissensions were not only scientific (chiefly about theories of disorder and at that stage, less about technique)—they were political, over power and control of the organization, and fueled by jealousies and rivalries. For the very notion of a movement is inherently political: it is a program defined by platforms and planks. Coeval with the birth of psychoanalysis, started by Jews, Breuer and Freud, for example, another movement, political Zionism, was being born, started by another Viennese Jew, Theodor Herzl (with whom Freud would occasionally exchange friendly nods on the same Berggasse where he lived and worked). The first congress of the latter movement was held in Basel in 1897 while Freud was busy writing his revolutionary The Interpretation of Dreams. The very notion of a movement ipso facto implies its social, cultural, and historical context. This is Eran Rolnik's guiding principle in Freud in Zion, particularly the inextricable bond between psychoanalytic science and psychoanalytic politics.

Rolnik, an Israeli psychiatrist, psychoanalyst, and historian, has given us a splendid, thoroughly researched book on the history of psychoanalysis as a sociopolitical movement against the backdrop of cultural and social trends in Jewish Palestine, from the end of World War I in 1918 to the early days of the establishment of the state of Israel in 1948. responseSet.title: Primary Mental Expression: Freud, Klein, and Beyond
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Primary Mental Expression: Freud, Klein, and Beyond

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins

56 Kestrel Lane
Amherst, MA 01002
E-mail: mdrobbinsmd@comcast.net

Michael Robbins   Boston Psychoanalytic Society.

Freud formulated the primary process model, describing mental activity that creates the illusion of an actual experience in lieu of reflective thought, at the very start of his career. In this, his initial formulation of unconscious mental activity, he was attempting to account for the nature of dreaming, by inference for the mind of infancy and, more speculatively, for adult psychosis. He never revised the model in light of his later formulations of the structural model and the death instinct, nor did he elaborate on his speculation that it could serve as a model for psychosis, and there has been little subsequent effort to employ the model outside the context of dreaming. A small number of analysts, including Klein, Bion, and Matte-Blanco, have constructed theories of psychosis in idiosyncratic conceptual languages that seem to be describing phenomena similar to those from which Freud constructed his model. Although Klein's model of positions, which has become the most widely accepted theory of psychosis, is generally considered a fundamental departure from Freud, both accounts have remarkable similarity and both tend to confuse primary mental expression with mature thought and normal infancy with psychosis. Contributions by cognitive-developmental psychologists including Werner and Piaget suggest ways to clarify some of the confusion and to supplement and amplify Freud's and Klein's description of some of the salient features of primary mental expression. Findings from neuroimaging studies of dreaming and of schizophrenia support the proposition that primary mental activity is a qualitatively distinctive form of mental expression.

responseSet.title: Multiple Models in Clinical Practice: Bane or Blessing?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Multiple Models in Clinical Practice: Bane or Blessing?

Dale Panzer

Dale Panzer

43 Leopard Road, Suite 101
Paoli, PA 19301
E-mail: dalepanzer@comcast.net

Dale Panzer  

The use of multiple models in the clinical situation—a bane or a blessing? That was the question posed by Sydney Pulver, who chaired this panel, to set the stage for the audience. While acknowledging that this may be “too extreme a question,” Pulver underscored its importance, given that psychoanalysis is currently “in a theoretical state … characterized by pluralism.”

We have many different schools and many different ways of thinking about psychoanalysis. From the standpoint of the development and growth of psychoanalytic theory, is it good for us to have multiple models? Is it useful? Pulver raised these questions and then let the audience know we would not be dealing with them today. Instead the panel would look at multiple models from the clinical standpoint. That is, it would consider what stance the clinician should take and how the use of multiple models impacts the clinical work for good or ill.

Pulver and Glen Gabbard, the scientific program chair, assembled a panel of eminent clinicians to answer these therapeutic questions. Sander Abend, who identifies himself as an exponent of modern conflict theory, was selected to represent the position that one comprehensive theory remains optimal in the clinical setting. Ronald Britton, from England, who identifies himself as a post-Kleinian, was selected to represent his work with multiple models and perhaps to discuss how the pluralistic milieu in contemporary British psychoanalysis impacts his clinical work. Fred Pine acknowledges Freudian roots but does not identify himself with any school or theory. He was invited as a longstanding advocate of the clinical use of multiple models who has written extensively on the subject. Pulver said that Pine had hinted to him that he would say something that would be a variation on his previous views. Pulver added that he was not certain that any of the three would in fact take these positions;

Panel held at the American Psychoanalytic Association Winter Meeting, New York, January 21, 2006. Panelists: Sydney Pulver (chair), Sander Abend, Ronald Britton, Fred Pine. Presenter: Linda Spero.

[[RunningHead]]

Memory and Modernism

Robert Pinsky

Robert Pinsky

8 Maple Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: rpinsky@comcast.net

Robert Pinsky  

It was an honor (and a little intimidating) to address a plenary session of the American Psychoanalytic Association last January. The effort to say something useful from the viewpoint of poetry about the discipline of analysis has contributed to my thinking about my art, and I am grateful for that.

A personal custom of speaking to an audience, rather than reading from a text, means there is no written version of the talk. As with many forms of human speech and conversation, perhaps including both of the disciplines involved, a literal transcript might be distorting, in any case. Any transcript leaves out the corporeal part of discourse—which poetry strives to re-create as its end, and where psychoanalysis, as I understand it, begins.

So instead, here is a summary of the presentation, which involved a general proposition and a series of examples. The effort was to see psychoanalysis—in its roots and also in its recent stresses, doubts, and internal debates—as part of modernism, and therefore subject to the still-evolving cultural fate of modernism. I tried to take a step toward seeing the internal debates of poetry (which also has its stresses, doubts, and internal debates) and psychoanalysis, both, as reflecting the fate of modernism as a radical and perpetually resisted mode of memory.

The first example was a two-line poem by the nineteenth-century poet Walter Savage Landor:

On love, on grief, on every human thing,

Time sprinkles Lethe's water with his wing.

This easily memorizable poem embodies a contradiction—in part, precisely because it is so memorizable. The lines about universal forgetting quite literally embody

Professor of English and Creative Writing, Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, Boston University; U.S. Poet Laureate 1997-2000; founder, Favorite Poem Project.

Submitted for publication May 7, 2008.

responseSet.title: Six Essays on Sexuality: An Introduction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Six Essays on Sexuality: An Introduction

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

E-mail: rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

One hundred years ago it was crystal clear: “Sexuality … provides the motive power for every single symptom, and for every single manifestation of a symptom. The symptoms of the disease are nothing else than the patient's sexual activity. … I never find it otherwise—that sexuality is the key to the problem of the psychoneuroses and of the neuroses in general” (Freud 1905a [“Dora”], p. 115).

Then these were invariant laws of nature: every neurotic symptom is actuated by sexuality and every symptom represents compromised sexuality. In today's world we assume that no laws are invariant and question whether there is such a thing as “Nature.” In recent years the leading edge of psychoanalytic thought has not been focused on sexuality. In the plenary address that leads off this issue of JAPA, Peter Fonagy refers to our neglect of the subject as long an “open secret.” Yet psychoanalysis, despite this hiatus of recent decades, has hardly given up on its quest to understand sexuality.

In Three Essays Freud (1905b) described the movement of the libido with the metaphor of a waterway whose main channel is impeded (p. 170). The blocked waters first back up, then proceed forward in multiple more or less parallel streams. Progress is achieved, to varying degrees, by flow in the collateral channels. These actions may themselves describe the mode of advance of recent psychoanalytic theory: there is no longer a clearly defined mainstream. While our energies are divided, our present theoretical complexity may be better suited to the objects of our investigations. Sexuality may more readily reveal its remaining mysteries to gentle insinuations from multiple sources than to a continued frontal assault.

In addition to Fonagy's plenary, we have gathered in the pages to follow five new essays on the theory of sexuality that approach our subject from varying psychoanalytic points of view. We have in Fonagy's essay an approach from the angle of attachment, informed and inspired as it is also by the French “neo-Copernican” revolution identified with Laplanche.1

[[RunningHead]]

What can We Learn from Second Analyses?

Kathleen Lyon

Kathleen Lyon

80 Fifth Avenue, Suite 1005
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: lyon61@msn.com

Kathleen Lyon  

Chair Gerald Fogel said he had convened this panel to take up the question of what second analyses can tell us about analytic process, both its potential and its limitations. This question, he noted, had not been addressed at a meeting of the American for many years, during which time significant changes were occurring in the profession. One of these changes is the ascendance of a two-person psychology as an orienting perspective for practitioners. Another important development is the much greater openness analysts now have regarding their need for continued self-reflection and self-analysis. Analysts feel much freer to discuss their countertransference reactions and to consider without shame the possibility of embarking on their own second analysis. Given that the scant literature on reanalysis focuses almost exclusively on the mistakes of the first analyst and the question of the analysand's analyzability, Fogel found good reason to think that a panel of contemporary analytic thinkers, from varying backgrounds and orientations, would generate new and useful insights. Dominique Scarfone, a training and supervising analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute in Montreal, is in the tradition of French analytic training. Theodore Jacobs is from the NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; his broad North American viewpoint is influenced by a traditional ego psychological approach and by an interest in the use of countertransference and the self in treatment. Maxine Anderson has trained both at the Seattle Institute and in England and currently is influenced greatly by Kleinian/Bionian theory and technique.

Jacobs's contribution, “On Reanalysis,” dealt extensively with the experience of the current analyst's countertransferences toward the previous analyst (or analysts). He opened with a description of his first analyst. As a college student he began an analysis with an analyst he said “would

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 21, 2007. Panelists: Gerald Fogel (chair), Dominique Scarfone, Theodore J. Jacobs, Maxine Anderson.

responseSet.title: Freud's Little Oedipus: Hans as Exception to the Oedipal Rule
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Little Oedipus: Hans as Exception to the Oedipal Rule

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

30412 South State Street
Ann Arbor, Ml 48104
E-mail: k.ahbel-rappe@sbcglobal.net

Karin Ahbel-Rappe   Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Council.

Freud's “The Analysis of a Phobia in a Five-Year-Old Boy” is regarded by Freud and by analytic readers and commentators as a prototype of his conception of the oedipus complex. A literary methodology is used to show that the interpretation of the oedipus complex at work in Freud's text in fact differs from Freud's standard view of it. While studying the paper as text, not as case report, may obscure or distort some clinical matters, it is valuable in that it makes legible a sort of theoretical unconscious in the text. In contrast to Freud's typically tragic view of the oedipus complex (in the tradition of ancient Greek tragedy), the Hans study evokes a comic vision (in the tradition of Greek New Comedy). This comic vision allows Hans a happy imaginative ending to the oedipal dilemma, challenges certain epistemic pretensions, and emphasizes the oedipus complex as a set of abiding existential questions. Given the deep link between Freud”s oedipus concept and a tragic view of human life, this departure in the Hans paper is a fascinating anomaly.

[[RunningHead]]

Loewald Panel: Discussion

Ellen Pinsky

Ellen Pinsky

8 Maple Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: epinsky@comcast.net

Ellen Pinsky  

In one and the same act—I am tempted to say, in the same breath and the same sucking of milk—drive direction and organization of environment into shapes or configurations begin.

—Hans Loewald (1960, p. 238)

Lively, readable, and personal, these four essays are not standard psychoanalytic papers. Moving narratives of appreciation, they honor Hans Loewald as a psychoanalytic thinker whose work stands with, extends, and completes Freud's. Here, for example, is Nancy Chodorow in her introduction: “Loewald's writing, in its depth and range if not its quantity, approaches Freud's; in its conceptual clarity, precision, and elegance, it is unmatched” (p. 1091). Ted Jacobs writes that “Loewald's formulations opened up a way of thinking in classical analysis that recognized the importance of the interactive dimension in analysis …” (p. 1099). Larry Friedman sums up, “[W]hat Loewald did for me was to provide a convincing rationale for an interpretive talking treatment” (p. 1108). And Rosemary Balsam predicts that Loewald, “because of the open, integrative nature of his thought, and its abiding clinical aptness, will only gain in stature in the years to come” (p. 1117).

Each panelist in some form addresses the same question: How did I come to Loewald and what did he give me? I will center my own, somewhat personal, response around the idea of safekeeping, as that idea is related to Loewald's distinctive way of writing about psychoanalysis—a style illustrated in the epigraph above by the contrast between the organic sucking and breathing on one side and the systematic organization and configuration on the other: polarities balanced on the marvelously colloquial fulcrum of “I am tempted to say.” Like Freud, Loewald offers “not prescriptions but “guiding principles” (Balsam, p. 1123); and like Freud, too, he has power as a writer.

A thinker and a caregiver, Loewald trained first as a philosopher and second in medicine. Safekeeping is also double: both an abstract idea

Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

responseSet.title: The Essence of Hans Loewald
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Essence of Hans Loewald

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam

64 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: rosemary.balsam@yale.edu

Rosemary Balsam  

Hans Loewald, like Freud, is a beloved figure for many, and those of us who have been affected by his profound teaching seem each to have emerged with a different slant on what should be regarded as his most important contributions. My Loewald may not be others' Loewald. Perhaps that rich multidimensionality is itself the hallmark of a seminal thinker. Certainly the same could be said for the work of Freud himself or, say, Ferenczi, Jung, Klein, Fairbairn, Kohut, or Winnicott, whereas one would not say it of Mahler, Greenacre, Rapaport, or Paul Gray, fine contributors to our field though they have been. Loewald's work has successfully transcended the era of theoretical pluralism. I predict that because of the open, integrative nature of his thought, and its abidingly clinical aptness, he will only gain in stature in the years to come. Arnold Cooper (1991) has appreciated him, as has Joel Whitebook (2004), as (to use the latter's phrase) a “radical conservative.” Given his importance, many have wondered why there is no “Loewaldian” school.

He was a slightly built man, a quiet presence who often sat crunched up as he listened to case material, his hand on his forehead like Rodin's Le Penseur; a man given to receptive, reactive listening, with an economical but incisive flow of words, a capacity to be blunt and not waste time beating about the bush. He frightened some with his air of austerity, but he also had a naughty twinkle and a great readiness to see humor. I and many others, I think, felt very free in his presence in spite of—or perhaps, in my case, because of—his gentlemanly, old-world formality. He carried weight for me like my old professors from medical school in the UK, whom I first encountered at the impressionable age of seventeen. He conveyed an air of such interest in one's thinking that I felt that what I said to him was almost magically dignified into something far more fascinating than I had appreciated.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guilt

Anna Aragno

Anna Aragno

Montclair, NJ 07043
E-mail: oppenheimL@mail.montclair.edu

Anna Aragno  

Guilt is one of those amorphous words that twist and turn in the mind as they try to find a defining shape and all too often misidentify themselves as something they are not, in this instance, all too often, shame. As Irene Cairo explained in her introduction to this panel, it is the function of guilt that differentiates it from shame, and there is an important distinction to be made as well between “bad” guilt and “good.” Indeed, that distinction (the complexity of which she illustrated with two hypothetical “clinical cases,” Lady Macbeth for the bad and the Roman emperor Hadrian for the good) was the philosophical frame of the panel. Seeking to redress what Cairo deemed a wrong inflicted of late on the affect of guilt, the panel sought to identify the reasons for a decreased interest in guilt, as well as to uncover its multiple sources and the various ways it is dealt with in analytic treatment. Cairo wondered, for example, if the diminished interest in guilt in deference to shame might have to do, culturally speaking, with “an avoidance of addressing experiences that point toward assuming responsibility for ourselves, not just our actions but our minds.”

The panelists, Arnold Rothstein, Estelle Shane, and Virginia Ungar, represented diverse theoretical perspectives, the Freudian, the self psychological, and the Kleinian respectively. This lent an important measure of diversity to the presentations and the discussion. The format of the panel was as follows: In advance of the meeting, Cairo had posed the panelists a series of three questions (or sets of questions) to which they were to prepare responses. The first was diagnostic: “How do you detect unconscious guilt—in symptoms, in dreams, in defenses, as a compulsion to repeat?” The second was more developmental: “Do you think guilt and shame are comparable as moral regulators? Do you see the comparison as useful in childhood development? In adult functioning?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Bell

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

Karin Ahbel-Rappe

30412 South State Street
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: k.ahbel-rappe@sbcglobal.net

Karin Ahbel-Rappe  

I make my living as a clinical psychoanalyst. When I read Freud from that perspective, I read his case reports as records of analytic treatments, as part of the project of being better informed about the history of psychoanalytic theory and technique, of being a better clinician. The same point of view takes the lead when I teach Freud to candidates and other students in the context of an analytic institute. The clinical project is at the heart of my professional life. And the distinguished Silvia Bell offers many pertinent and sensitive ideas regarding Freud's Hans paper as a case study. In the particular essay under discussion, however, I attempt a different project—with a different methodology, different way of thinking, different results. Because Bell does not take this difference into proper account, her criticisms of my paper mostly miss the mark.

Bell complains that I “make no mention of the paper's…function…as a report of the analytic treatment of a child.” She calls this a “flagrant omission given the title Freud gave to his text—‘An Analysis of a Phobia…”’ (p. 868). In fact, my “omission” is an intentional methodological bracketing of the official function of Freud's paper as a case report, in order to explore it from another point of view, as a text. My intent is to add to an understanding of the complexity of Freud's paper by showing that it serves functions in addition to (not instead of) the ones that were at the front of his mind. My exploration is of the way that, beneath Freud's professional (and thoroughly appropriate) purposes—to record a treatment, to study child development, to focus theoretically on the centrality of the tragic oedipus complex—a different theoretical notion of the complex is at play. To put it another way, exploring papers as texts opens us to the ways they differ from their official functions.

Similarly, Bell complains that I disregard evidence “that the form [of Freud's paper], which involves the reconstructed dialogue, is dictated to Freud by the data he received from the father” (p. 867) and that Freud adhered to this form because he wanted to make public a powerful set of data about the centrality of the oedipus complex. I am well aware of these points.

Ritual and Its Consequences: An Essay on theLimits of Sincerity. By Adam P. Seligman, Robert P. Weller, Michael J. Puett, and Bennett Simon. New York: Oxford University Press, 2008, 248 pp., $99.00.

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

258 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: pschwaber@wesleyan.edu

Paul Schwaber  

This is an engaging and fascinating study, remarkably ranging in its purview—at times breathtakingly so. Written jointly by three professors (of religion, anthropology, and Chinese history) and a psychoanalyst, it sustains an impressive and somewhat surprising thesis. Ritual, they argue, easily disavowed or distrusted in modern times, is endemic to the very way human beings address life and extend into the world, because even as ritual consists of insistent repetitions, it maintains continuities and ventilates possibilities.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Language of Empathy: An Analysis of its Constitution, Development, and Role in Psychoanalytic Listening

Anna Aragno

Anna Aragno

140–142 West End Avenue, #23C
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Annaragno@earthlink.net

Anna Aragno   Private practice, New York.

Viewed from an epistemological perspective, empathy in psychoanalytic practice is described as that aspect of a specialized attentional stance that opens channels of interaction facilitating the formation of a trusting bond and enabling one to gain access to the emotional qualities of another's experience. A literature overview traces the origins of the concept in Freud and its role in psychoanalytic listening (including its controversial, divisive evolution in our field). Empathy is then examined from a semiotic-developmental framework. Its constitutional origins, differentiated forms, and distinctive purpose in clinical discourse are discussed. A developmental line is proposed, and clear distinctions are drawn between empathy in everyday life and its specialized technical application in clinical work.

[[RunningHead]]

The Interplay of Genes, Environments, and Psychoanalysis

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

E-mail: shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

We begin the 2008 JAPA Research Sections with a window onto a realm of contemporary research that holds great, but as yet almost completely untapped, value for psychoanalysis. In our featured paper, Suzanne Haddad and her colleagues serve as welcome guides to the rapidly expanding world of gene and environment research (“nature-nurture interplay”)—an interdisciplinary domain currently being pursued with stunning innovation, vigor, and energy (see Kendler and Prescott 2006; Rutter 2007; Torgeson 2006). The great promise—in both findings and methods—of the new genetics studies has been recognized, often with well-deserved fanfare, by our colleagues in psychiatry, neuroscience, and the behavioral sciences (e.g., Caspi et al. 2002, 2003; Rutter 2006). Haddad and colleagues now cogently argue for yet another new bridge—from the insights of behavior genetics studies to psychoanalytic perspectives and interventions.

In recent years, papers addressing neuroscience interfaces with psychoanalysis and other disciplines—social cognition, developmental psychology—have appeared in JAPA and elsewhere (see, e.g., Gerber and Peterson 2006, in press; Mayes 2003; Peterson et al. 2007; Roffman and Gerber 2008). With rare exceptions (e.g., Mayes 2003), contemporary research addressing genetics and human development, personality, psychopathology, and environmental experience has received scant attention in psychoanalytic quarters. This benign neglect is not surprising, in light of genetics studies' inherently complex constructs and methods, and their vocabulary, so unfamiliar to most psychoanalysts and mental health researchers.

These dissemination and translation difficulties are compounded, as Hadddad correctly notes, by the fact that most genetics studies involve abstract constructs applying to classes of variables, use large samples, and address but a few key dimensions.

509

Summaries of the Sixth Annual Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association: Part I

Distinguishing Between Shyness and Social Phobia in College Students

Mia Biran and Annie Ozarchuk

Annie Ozarchuk

Miami University, Ohio

114 Psychology Building
Miami University
Oxford, OH 45056
E-mail: biranmw@muohio.edu

Annie Ozarchuk  

Social phobia is the persistent fear of scrutiny in social or performance situations (DSM-IV). The study of social phobia is especially relevant in college populations because of the scrutiny that takes place in the classroom and in oral presentations, the development of new relationships, and the social interactions necessary to launch a career after graduation.

The terms shyness and social phobia are often used synonymously in the literature. Advertisements for subjects in social anxiety studies often ask for “extremely shy people” (Rettew 2000). However, social phobia and shyness are not the same thing. Social phobia is a clinical disorder, whereas shyness is a normative temperamental construct marked by discomfort in social situations (Heiser, Turner, and Beidel 2002). Nevertheless, the two share many characteristics (Chavira, Stein, and Malcarne 2002): because shyness and social phobia are so closely related, understanding the relationship between the two may aid in the development of psychodynamic treatment plans for college students suffering from social inhibitions.

There are several hypotheses about the relationship between shyness and social phobia, ranging from the idea that shyness and social phobia are completely different constructs, to the idea that they are the same construct differing on a continuum of severity (Heiser, Turner, and Beidel 2002). In a study of possible developmental factors leading to social phobia, Stemberger et al. (1995)

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Mind

Revolution in Mind: The Creation of Psychoanalysis. By George Makari. New York: Harper Collins, 2008, 613 pp., $32.50.

Leon Wurmser

Leon Wurmser

904 Crestwick Road
Towson, MD 21286
E-mail: leonwurmser@copper.net

Leon Wurmser  

This volume contains an incredible amount of knowledge regarding various attempts over the past hundred years to systematically unravel the mysteries of subjectivity, to conjoin the intricacy of psychology with the objectivity of science. Many are the offspring of that shotgun marriage: some forsook all efforts at consensual validation in favor of speculative insight or the breadth of comprehensive theory; others stressed objectification and a narrowly conceived “empiricism” to the detriment of giving full value to the individual's inner life. There has been an incessant fluidity between these two poles. The endeavor clearly can survive only in a spirit of complementarity: only an insistent effort to reconcile the need to do justice to the richness of subjectivity with the imperative to establish transpersonal laws, to combine the insights of the clinical case and the personal truth with the construction of theories, can approximately fulfill the task of understanding and treating the mind. What remains necessary is a back-and-forth between two approaches to Being: the I-You relationship and the I-It experience, to use Martin Buber's concepts.

George Makari, a psychoanalyst and professor of psychiatry and its history, has written a wonderfully comprehensive work that describes in a spellbinding, even passionate, way the history of this search roughly between 1850 and 1950. It is by no means a new biography of Freud, though Freud's personality, his struggles, and the philosophical and practical (even political) shifts in his career have had to take center stage: “this is less the story of one man than it is the history of a series of heated intellectual contests. … Sigmund Freud did not so much create a revolution in the way men and women understood their inner lives. Rather, he took command of revolutions that were already in progress” (pp. 4–5).

Psychopathology and Treatment

Handbook of Dynamic Psychotherapy for Higher Level Personality Pathology. By Eve Caligor, Otto F. Kernberg, and John F. Clarkin. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Publishing, 2007, 284 pp., $65.00.

Richard Fox

Richard Fox

226 Monarch Bay
Dana Point, CA 92629
E-mail: rpfoxmd@aol.com

Richard Fox  

In this new handbook, Eve Caligor, Otto Kernberg, and John Clarkin present a detailed exposition of how contemporary object relations theory can be applied systematically in an intensive psychotherapy for patients presenting with the rigidity that characterizes higher-level personality pathology. It is a companion to an earlier handbook (Clarkin, Yeoman, and Kernberg 2006) on transference-focused psychotherapy, or TFP. Both therapies are based on Kernberg's “structural assessment of personality” (1984). Their approach is not “one size fits all” but rather an attempt to develop treatments tailored to specific psychopathology.

In a brief concluding chapter, the authors note the potential value of the handbook as a manual for research, as well as a text for teaching and learning psychotherapy. It incorporates a clearly defined theory of psychotherapeutic technique and therapeutic change. The current volume shares both the advantages and the limitations of a procedural manual.

It is unfortunate that this dual purpose was not stated more clearly at the onset, since the text may be read from either a research or a clinical/teaching perspective. I will focus my comments on the latter frame of reference and leave the research perspective to qualified investigators. However, it is my sense that the research emphasis has lessened the book's clinical richness.

For the clinician, theory and technique flow from the individual patient, and, while the authors discuss patient assessment and differential treatment planning, they do so only in the ninth of twelve chapters, more than two-thirds of the way through the book.

1053

The Theory and Treatment of Depression: Towards a Dynamic Interactionism Model. Edited by Jozef Corveleyn, Patrick Luyten, and Sidney J. Blatt. Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates, 2005, 304 pp., $37.50.

Fredric Busch

Fredric Busch

10 East 78th Street, #5A
New York, NY 10075
E-mail: FNB80@aol.com

Fredric Busch  

With the advent of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual, third edition, and the increasing use of psychopharmacological interventions for psychiatric disorders, depression was increasingly viewed as a biological illness requiring medication as the primary intervention. Over time, short-term manualized psychotherapeutic interventions such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and interpersonal therapy (IPT) were developed and found to be efficacious. Although these treatments brought psychological elements back into consideration, they were also taken as indicators that psychoanalytic interventions were unnecessary and/or not useful for depression.

The Theory and Treatment of Depression: Towards a Dynamic Interactionism Model provides a refreshing corrective to these perceptions. Over time, as noted in the introduction, depression proved more complex to treat than initially thought, as even with medication and brief psychotherapeutic interventions many patients showed incomplete response or recurrence. The authors suggest that this vulnerability to depression is due to a complex array of factors, including psychodynamic, cognitive, and personality dimensions. In addition, depressive disorders have a greater degree of hetereogeneity than what is implied in the DSM.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation, Sexuality, and Adaptation: Normalization in Adolescence

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

525 East 68th Street
P.O. Box 140
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: tshapiro@med.Cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro   Emeritus Professor, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, Columbia Psychoanalytic Clinic.

During adolescence the central masturbation fantasy that is formulated during childhood takes its final form and paradoxically must now be directed outward for appropriate object finding and pair matching in the service of procreative aims. This is a step in adaptation that requires a further developmental landmark that I have called normalization. The path toward airing these private fantasies is facilitated by chumship relationships as a step toward further exposure to the social surround. Hartmann's structuring application of adaptation within psychoanalysis is used as a framework for understanding the process that simultaneously serves intrapsychic and social demands and permits goals that follow evolutionary principles. Variations in the normalization process from masturbatory isolation to a variety of forms of sexual socialization are examined in sociological data concerning current adolescent sexual behavior and in case examples that indicate some routes to normalized experience and practice.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: The Loewaldian Legacy

Nancy Chodorow

Nancy Chodorow

7 Meadow Way
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: nancy_chodorow@hms.harvard.edu

Nancy Chodorow  

Probably every American analyst has read, in training and many times since, “On the Therapeutic Action of Psychoanalysis” (Loewald 1960). This groundbreaking paper, certainly one of the most important papers of the second half of the twentieth century, is part of the oeuvre of an analyst who contributed original writings on almost every topic of psychoanalytic theory, metapsychology, and epistemology, on the clinical relationship, on therapeutic action and its failures, and on development. In this paper Loewald gives us the evocative image of analysis as the project of turning ghosts into ancestors. He holds onto a radically challenging vision of transference, in which transference is “pathological insofar as the unconscious is a crowd of ghosts” (p. 249) but in which reality is pathological insofar as it doesn't include transference. As Loewald puts it: “Without such transference—of the intensity of the unconscious, of the infantile ways of experiencing life that have no language and little organization, but the indestructibility and power of the origins of life—to the preconscious and to present-day life and contemporary objects, human life becomes sterile and an empty shell…. our present, current experiences have intensity and depth to the extent to which they are in communication (interplay) with the unconscious, infantile, experiences representing the indestructible matrix of all subsequent experiences” (pp. 250-251).

The paper argues for the interactional basis of analytic investigation and the analytic relationship—for the analyst as new object and as “co-actor on the analytic stage.” It deals with “the central problem of the relationship between the development of psychic structures and interaction with other structures … the connection between ego formation and object relations” (p. 221). The analyst, then, is not simply a “reflecting mirror characterized by scrupulous neutrality” (p. 223)

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow

2444 Wilshire Boulevard, #624
Santa Monica, CA 90403
E-mail: robertdstolorow@gmail.com

Robert Stolorow  

From time to time a book appears in the field of philosophy that holds great relevance for contemporary psychoanalysis. One such book is Subjectivity and Selfhood by Dan Zahavi, professor of philosophy and

1039

director of the Center for Subjectivity Research at the University of Copenhagen. In this book Zahavi provides valuable philosophical tools that promise to be of help in clearing up conceptual muddles about “the self” that pervade contemporary psychoanalytic discourse.

Zahavi's aim is to investigate the interrelationships among experience, self-awareness, and selfhood, in support of the claim that selfhood cannot be comprehended apart from a form of self-awareness intrinsic to the structure of subjectivity itself. In the course of his investigation he gives the reader a grand tour of the works of the major contributors to philosophical phenomenology—Brentano, Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Ricoeur—along with a smattering of contemporary philosophers of mind. Among the topics covered are the first-person perspective, structures of experience and prereflective self-awareness, intentionality and self-acquaintance, temporality and time-consciousness, embodiment and body-awareness, and empathy and intersubjectivity. Zahavi's elucidations of difficult philosophical works are exceptionally clear and accessible; indeed, portions of his book could serve as an excellent, reader-friendly introduction to philosophical phenomenology.

Of particular importance for contemporary psychoanalysis is Zahavi's clear delineation of three distinctive conceptions of self found in philosophical discourse:

The Kantian self. The self of Kantian philosophy is not directly experienced; it is the inferred locus of identity in the midst of changing experiences. Our changing experiences all have something in common: they all have the same subject, they are all lived through by one and the same self. The Kantian self remains one and the same through time. This selfsame subject, according to Kant, stands apart from our experiences and constitutes their unity and coherence. Although Zahavi does not make this point, the Kantian subject seems also to be the agent of choice and action.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Loewald's “Binocular Vision” and the Art of Analysis

J. David Miller

J. David Miller

3 Washington Circle NW, Suite 405
Washington, DC 20037
E-mail: jdmiller1957@earthlink.net

J. David Miller  

In his 1988 monograph on sublimation, Hans Loewald describes the process as a transformation of the drives aimed at re-creating what he presumes to be the subjective experience of infantile attachment. To describe this experience, he invokes a state of mind that he calls “binocular vision.” He maintains that this mental state may arise not only in activities usually associated with sublimation, such as the creation and enjoyment of art, but in all forms of sublimation, including effective psychoanalysis. Because Loewald's discussion is largely theoretical, it does not convey how the concept of binocular vision may inform clinical technique and interdisciplinary study. A comparative application of his theory to psychoanalytic process and to the viewer's response to art enables one to grasp binocular vision as a common aspect of both. It emerges as a useful model with which to conceptualize and integrate the ambiguous reality of analytic experience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writing as Psychoanalytic Pedagogy: A Primer (The Columbia Psychoanalytic Writing Program)

Robert Glick

Robert Glick

E-mail: rag4@columbia.edu

Robert Glick  

If, as Freud suggested, psychoanalysis is one of the “impossible professions,” along with education and government, then teaching and learning clinical psychoanalysis are doubly, and, given the institutional and organizational challenges, triply, impossible. Embarking on a psychoanalytic education can be a daunting endeavor for students and teachers alike.

Psychoanalytic education has always been a work in progress, with an optimistic hope of continued improvement, but no ideal method in sight. Our educational models began as personal mentorships, moved on to guild apprenticeships, and then, in 1920, to formal educational standardization that sought to define the criteria and boundaries of the profession. Since then, formal structured training institutions have dominated our field—the Eitingon model becoming the standard for much of an organized and increasingly professionalized psychoanalysis.

The pedagogy of the Eitingon and French models—the two current paradigms—places most of the burden (the training analysis aside) on supervision as the transmitter of psychoanalytic knowledge and skill. The didactic curriculum, once the heart of psychoanalytic teaching, has devolved into an undervalued role as a mode of learning about analytic process, therapeutic change, and the analyst's participation. Generally, the lived, personalized experience of the candidate is treated delicately in supervision, and from a safe distance in most process courses.

This tradition has conspired to underutilize writing as an effective mode of learning. Case summaries have often been an administrative task, and rarely exploited as an educational opportunity. The “talking cure” has largely been taught by the “talking training.”

Writing is an act of creativity and discovery.

Mortal Combat: The Tragic Vision of Philip Roth

The Dying Animal. By Philip Roth. Boston and New York: Houghton Mifflin, 2001, 176 pp., $23.00.Everyman. By Philip Roth. Boston and New York: Houghton Mifflin, 2006, 192 pp., $24.00.

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: hblevine@aol.com

Howard Levine  

Philip Roth is a trenchant, witty, and psychologically sophisticated observer of the male psyche and the American cultural scene. Though Roth is perhaps best known for his comic portrayal of raw male sexual desire in such novels as Portnoy's Complaint or his depictions of the vicissitudes of the American Jewish experience (American Pastoral, The Plot against America), two recently published novels, The Dying Animal and Everyman, show that he is equally adept at addressing the complexities that surround the ultimate questions of love and loss, living and dying. His earlier work has always reflected his interest in the claims and desires of “the human body (usually male) in its strength, its frailty, and its often ridiculous need” (Pierpont 2006, p. 82), but in these two novels he examines the ultimate endgame of life, the point at which the manic defenses of erotic pleasure seeking begin perforce to wane, along with one's power, strength, and vigor, sexual and otherwise. For psychoanalysts, in particular, these two novels should be of special interest for the insight they offer and the questions they raise about the all-too-human conflicts of commitment vs. freedom, attachment vs. Eros, and ultimately about the struggle between reality and illusion and the role played by sexuality and sensuousness in our attempts to both deal with and evade the inevitability of mortality, death, and decline.

In The Dying Animal

1An aged man is but a paltry thing,A tattered coat upon a stick, unlessSoul clap its hands and sing. …Consume my heart away; sick with desireAnd fastened to a dying animalIt knows not what it is. …283

[[RunningHead]]

Pluralism in Action

David Brendel

David Brendel

115 Mill Street
Belmont, MA 02478
E-mail: dbrendel@partners.org

David Brendel  

This panel addressed the challenge of translating conceptual pluralism in psychoanalytic theory into practical action in the consulting room. Steven Cooper introduced the panel by noting that the analyst functions in a state of uncertainty about what is going on with the patient, particularly at the beginning of a treatment, and that the clinician's theory may be stretched or called into question by the patient as the treatment proceeds. The analyst, therefore, ought to regard underlying theoretical presumptions as tentative and open to revision as the treatment proceeds—or as Ronald Britton has said, our theories should be considered “vulnerable knowledge.” Cooper suggested that the analyst necessarily establishes a dyadic relationship with his preferred theory and its limitations. He always should be open, however, to recognizing a “pluralistic third,” which Cooper views as an alternative conceptual model for understanding the patient and the complexities of the clinical interaction. Acknowledging the existence and critical role of the pluralistic third involves a de-idealization of the analyst's preferred theoretical approach, but is required to provide appropriate treatment and to function in a profession where our theories have become so deeply interpenetrated.

Cooper proceeded to describe a clinical case in which a single conceptual model was insufficient and introduction of a pluralistic third helped to move the analysis forward. Simon was a bright and likable, yet hostile and contemptuous, thirty-year-old man who had recently completed a year of psychoanalysis in another city, which Simon said had not gone well. In an early session with Cooper, Simon reported that he perceived Cooper as “an exceedingly earnest, caring analyst,” and he decided to call Cooper by the name Ernesto. Cooper responded, almost reflexively, that he would call Simon by the name Contempto.

responseSet.title: Getting Our Ideas Across: Three Very Different Views
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Getting Our Ideas Across: Three Very Different Views

Robert Glick

Robert Glick

E-mail: rag4@columbia.edu

Robert Glick  

Freud never said it would be easy getting our ideas across. Quite the contrary: the obstacles to being usefully heard, either in the consulting room or in the larger world, stand as the essential challenge in our psychoanalytic enterprise. We are consumed with finding out what is actually heard when we speak. However, we have also inherited the illusion that we are the profession of expert communicators—If we can't do it, no one can. In truth, we need all the help we can get, as analysts, as educators, and as purveyors of psychoanalytic knowledge in the marketplace of ideas. As purveyors of psychoanalytic knowledge, we can find our voices ignored, the value of our wares dismissed. Interest in psychoanalysis these days is quite paradoxical: in some ways we are a robust part of the culture's deep understanding of the life of the mind and its problems and treatments, while in other ways we are “old hat”—irrelevant, naive, unproven. Students at every level—from undergraduates to the candidates in our institutes—are no longer content to simply absorb received wisdom. They have become a challenging audience.

This issue's Education Section offers three very different explorations of the problems we face, as analysts and analytic educators, in getting our ideas across, of who our audiences are, and of the purposes to which psychoanalytic ideas may be applied.

In “Access to Psychoanalytic Ideas in American Undergraduate Institutions,” Jonathan Redmond and Michael Shulman present a survey undertaken to determine the availability of psychoanalytic knowledge in the course curricula of major U.S. colleges and universities. With financial support from the American Psychoanalytic Association's Task Force on Undergraduate Education and the International Psychoanalytical Association, the authors conducted a research project designed to answer the following questions: What knowledge of psychoanalysis is offered to undergraduates at 150 of the best institutions in the country? Where, in what departments, is anything about psychoanalysis offered? How does the country vary geographically in this availability?

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Practice and Theory

Practical, Psychoanalysis for Therapists and Patients. By Owen Renik. New York: Other Press, 2006, 179 pp, $24.00.

Ronald Benson

Ronald Benson

555 East William Street, Suite 23D
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: ronald.benson2@att.net

Ronald Benson  

Owen Renik has apparently written this brief book for psychoanalytically informed psychotherapists who are dynamically oriented but not formally trained in psychoanalysis and for patients who are currently in, or might be considering seeking, dynamically oriented treatment. Owen Renik is one of America's best known psychoanalysts. He is a training and supervising analyst at the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute, has been chair of the Program Committee and secretary of the Board on Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and was editor of Psychoanalytic Quarterly. His bibliography lists more than eighty publications on a variety of psychoanalytic topics. Perhaps he is best known for his series of papers on self-disclosure by the analyst in the analytic situation. Just recently, the New York Times prominently called attention to Renik's ideas in an article considering this book and consisting of an extended interview with the author (Carey 2006).

In this volume, he offers his opinion that analysts have grown too enamored with helping their patients seek insight and have lost their focus, more congruent with their patients' needs, toward seeking to achieve therapeutic benefit. This leads, he claims, to inefficiency and excessively long treatments. Therapeutic benefit is defined as relief of symptoms, and symptoms are whatever the patient feels is psychologically distressing. Perhaps this is so; or maybe not. His book is rich in clinical examples. His style is to make a bold and somewhat categorical assertion and then modify it with additional discussion and anecdotes to make it more nuanced. By this rhetorical device he grabs the reader's attention, raises anxiety and opposition, and in many instances, I believe, ultimately gains a consensus with the thoughtful reader. The reader is compelled to reconsider his or her own clinical experience, technique, and theoretical assumptions. Renik's entire oeuvre is characterized by this expository technique, but it is especially evident in Practical Psychoanalysis for Therapists and Patients.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depression and Internally Directed Aggression: Genetic and Environmental Contributions

Suzanne Haddad , David Reiss , Erica Spotts , Jody Ganiban , Paul Lichtenstein and Jenae Neiderhiser

Jenae Neiderhiser

1922 F Street NW, Suite 414 E
Washington, DC 20052
E-mail: suzannekerinhaddad@gmail.com

Jenae Neiderhiser   Liberal Arts Research Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychology, Pennsylvania State University.

This study uses behavior genetic (BG) methodology to investigate Freud's theory of depression as aggression directed toward the self (1930) and the extent to which genetically and environmentally influenced aggressive tendencies contribute to depressive symptoms. Data from the Twin and Offspring Study in Sweden (TOSS) is used to demonstrate how, in estimating shared and unique environmental influences, BG methods can inform psychoanalytic theory and practice, particularly because of their shared emphasis on the importance of individual experience in development. The TOSS sample consists of 909 pairs of adult twins, their partners, and one adolescent child per couple. The Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (Radloff 1977) was used to measure depressive symptoms and the Karolinska Scales of Personality (Schalling and Edman 1993) to measure internally directed aggression. Genetic analyses indicated that for both men and women, their unique experiences as well as genetic factors contributed equally to the association between internally directed aggression and depressive symptoms. These findings support Freud's theory that constitutionally based differences in aggression, along with individual experiences, contribute to a person's depressive symptoms. Establishing that an individual's unique, not shared, experiences and perceptions contribute to depressive symptoms and internally directed aggression reinforces the use of patient-specific treatment approaches implemented in psychoanalytic psychotherapy or psychoanalysis.

Sebastian Hartmann  

We are grateful for the opportunity to provide a short reply to Vittorio Gallese's comments on our paper “Empathy and Countertransference.” We have limited our response to five main points.

First, we do not object to a “broad” usage of the notion of empathy. Yet, like any science, psychoanalysis uses terms from ordinary language and specifies their meanings. It is the specific meaning of empathy in the language of psychoanalysis that we focus on in our paper.

Second, we do not in fact reduce empathy to the “psychology of emotions.” Gallese overlooks the fact that in reference to empathy we understand emotions as a means of gaining insight into the patient's psychic contents, which are meaningful in the sense that they relate to the patient's emotions.

Third, we reject the idea that there is a genetically prewired relationship between a specific facial expression and a specific affect. We also do not agree that mimetic display alone can afford any information about the mental contents that may possibly be reflected in that display; while Gallese maintains that there is such an invariant inter-individual relation between specific affects and specific facial displays and that the facial display alone suffices to inform one about a specific affect of the observed individual.

Fourth, we do not dispute the findings of infant research (as Gallese asserts); we simply deny the relevance of these findings as applied to psychoanalysis. As knowledge is always method-specific, and as the psychoanalytic method, with its tie to language (see, e.g., Freud 1916-1917, p. 17; see also Leavy 1973; Michels 1983), differs qualitatively from that of infant observation, the findings of infant research cannot be read directly in psychoanalytic contexts.

In Search of Psychoanalytic Truth

From Impression to Inquiry: A Tribute to the Work of Robert Wallerstein. Edited by Wilma Bucci and Norbert Freedman. London: International Psychoanalytical Association, 2007, 304 pp., $61.75.

Robert Holt

Robert Holt

Truro, MA 02666
E-mail: capebobholt @comcast.net

Robert Holt  

Robert S. Wallerstein—universally known as Bob—has been given a handsome and well-deserved tribute in this book, as well as in the celebratory conference of the Collaborative Analytic Multi-site Project (or CAMP) of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 2001, at which most of the papers collected here were presented. Unlike many a Festschrift, this one has enough solid substance to commend it to the attention of a wide readership.

Before the meaty chapters that summarize the present status of research on both the outcome and the process of psychoanalytic therapy, there is a group of short tributes, often telling how the author came to know and work with Bob. I have been invited to include a personal narrative of that kind, so I will imitate the structure of the book by reminiscing about my contacts with him before addressing the more substantive part of the book.

I had preceded Bob at Topeka by a few years, long enough to be trained in diagnostic testing by David Rapaport and then to be drawn into the research project he and Karl Menninger had begun, seeking to learn how to do a better job of selecting young doctors for psychiatric training. Soon I was joined by Lester Luborsky, and later a few other collaborators for shorter times. With Rapaport's encouragement, we launched into a reorganization of the Selection Project, as it was known. After three years of preparing a research design, with a specified set of aspects of personality and ability that were to be appraised by means of interviewing and testing tools we chose or devised, we were ready to try out our newly honed instruments on an incoming class of applicants for residency (Holt and Luborsky 1958).

997

Moral Stealth: How “Correct Behavior” Insinuates Itself into Psychotherapeutic Practice. By Arnold Goldberg. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2007, 144 pp., $32.00.

Theodore Mucha

Theodore Mucha

200 Retreat Avenue
Hartford, CT 06106
E-mail: tmucha@harthosp.org

Theodore Mucha  

In the introduction to his new book, Arnold Goldberg states that therapists, whatever their background and discipline, live in two separate worlds. The world of treatment is delineated by rules and regulations that are quite different from the rules and regulations that apply to social life.

1036

Goldberg's idea is that until the world of the therapist and that of social acquaintance are carefully separated and examined, difficulties will occur, especially in the area of ethics and morality. These difficulties exist to some degree in all practices, and we all share them, but it remains mostly unnoticed by most of us—hence the word “stealth” in the title of his book. Most analysts and therapists live and work in the zone between moral correctness and the desire to help their patients. The negotiation of this zone is the main theme of this book.

Goldberg is to be commended for pointing out that although The Principles of Medical Ethics, with Annotations Especially Applicable to Psychiatry, published by the American Psychiatric Association, and the American Psychoanalytic Association's Principles and Standards of Ethics for Psychoanalysts are necessary and helpful, they also can have adverse effects. They may restrict the psychoanalyst who submits to such standards from pursuing open inquiry in the analyses he or she conducts.

“Moral positions can have a profound impact on psychoanalysts' and psychotherapists' clinical practice without them being made explicit or often without our being aware of them. They have a ‘stealth-like’ existence and go undetected because of inattention. We need less to develop rules of proper behavior and guidelines for ethical practice (as important as they surely are), and more to recognize how unspoken and unrecognized moral postures have insinuated themselves in our daily practice. This may be more important than it seems, inasmuch as ultimately everything should be brought under the umbrella of our understanding whether we like it or not” (pp. 29–30).

Leon Hoffman  

An important task for psychoanalysts is how to make sense of the range and variety of current psychoanalytic theories in order to integrate clinical findings with systematic empirical findings and then, of course, with findings from cognitive and neuroscience. Two of our most senior and respected active colleagues, Charles Brenner1 and Leo Rangell (2007) have both forcefully presented arguments against the pluralism of psychoanalysis. Rangell recommends the development of a total composite theory through a “process of consolidation, aiming to achieve a livable togetherness of all valid contributed parts” (p. 86).

1This review is dedicated to the memory of Charles Brenner, who died May 19, 2008.1016

Edward Bibring Photographs the Psychoanalysts of His Time, 1932–1938. Edited by Sanford Gifford, Daniel Jacobs, Vivien Goldman, and the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2005, 206 pp., $49.95.

Moisy Shopper

Moisy Shopper

8820 Ladue Road
St. Louis, MO 63124
E-mail: mshopper@accessus.net

Moisy Shopper  

This small book is obviously a labor of love, infused with respect and historical sensibility. The photographs were taken by Edward Bibring with a Rolleiflex small enough to be used unobtrusively and almost candidly. They were taken at the IPA Congresses of 1932 in Wiesbaden, 1934 in Lucerne, 1936 in Marienbad, 1937 at the Vierlandertagung in Budapest, and 1938 in Paris.

I don't think that Edward Bibring had any aspirations to be an artistic photographer. The photos are strikingly ordinary, the sort the average tourist might take of friends and traveling companions when visiting a new city or when finding some interesting architectural background. Ordinary photographs of extraordinary people. While we no longer believe we can read personality from the bumps and irregularities of a person's head, I looked to these photos to offer specific insight into the character of the person photographed or some insight into the relationship of those framed together. I found myself scanning the faces, postures, and clothing of these icons of analysis, hoping to decipher some greater knowledge of these people. After this rather futile exercise, I found it more sensible to first view a photo and then imbue it with all the qualities of that person as he or she is known from scientific contributions, biographies, and analytic gossip.

315
