Psychoanalytic Disagreements in Context. By Dale Boesky. Lanham, MD: Jason Aronson, 2008, 228 pp., hardcover $80.00, paperback $39.95.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920
E-mail: judywall@mindspring.com

Robert Wallerstein  

This is an important and badly needed book, dealing as it does with a not just markedly unresolved, but an also strangely unattended, major handicap to both clinical and theoretical advance in psychoanalysis. What is centrally at issue here is the continuing problem in our psychoanalytic discourse of achieving consensual agreement in our understanding of the meanings of the clinical presentations in our consulting rooms, and of the formulation then of appropriate interpretations that will convey those understandings usefully to our patients.

However much our clinical and theoretical advances over recent decades have brought additional emphases in our conceptualization of mutative factors in our clinical endeavors, and have dislodged the zealous quest for veridical interpretation from its earlier primacy, as the almost unitary road to psychoanalytic change, we still, just about all of us, do, as Dale Boesky reminds us throughout this volume, offer interpretations to our patients, based on our presumed understanding of the presenting psychoanalytic situation, in our effort to help our patients understand themselves better. And we do this, all the while knowing that it is a commonplace of our field that different analysts regularly disagree in their conceptualization of what is salient in the clinical moment, whether under discussion in case conference, or presented in the literature.

1009

Philosophy, Ethics, and Psychoanalysis

Interpretation and Difference: The Strangeness of Care. By Alan Bass. Palo Alto: Stanford University Press, 2006, 216 pp., hardcover $68.00, paperback $25.95.

Ethan Kleinberg

Ethan Kleinberg

E-mail: ekleinberg@wesleyan.edu

Ethan Kleinberg  

Alan Bass's newest book, Interpretation and Difference: The Strangeness of Care, is a companion to his previous work, Difference and Disavowal: The Trauma of Eros (2000). While the earlier work was written “with an audience of psychoanalytic clinicians in mind,” Interpretation and Difference is “more specifically philosophical” (p. ix). Despite this distinction, Bass tells us in his introduction that the “two books are really one” (p. ix); this is an important point, because it leads us to the heart of Bass's project. In Difference and Disavowal Bass set out to explore what he considers a conundrum for clinical psychoanalysis, apparently analyzable patients who resist interpretation. In that work he allows that deconstructive thought is the means by which to shed light on this problem. In Interpretation and Difference he uses this clinical argument to engage the philosophers (Nietzsche, Heidegger, Derrida) and issues that underlie his reevaluation and revision of Freud. In this way, Bass's project is original, bold, and important both on its own terms and as a bridge between practicing psychoanalysts and contemporary theorists. To his credit, Bass manages to provide a sure path for both constituencies, though the terrain is difficult and Interpretation and Difference is by no means an easy read. I will return to this point. In essence, Bass is attempting to provide a practical therapeutic application for the thought of some of the more abstruse thinkers of the last 150 years. It is a testament to the author that the book succeeds on both the philosophical and the clinical level.

Interpretation and Difference begins by articulating the problem of the “concrete” (i.e., literal-minded) patient whose resistance to interpretation lights up the problem of interpretation in Freud and what Bass sees as some of the shortcomings of practicing analysts.

1029

Motherland of Psychoanalysis. By Thomas Freeman. Edited by John Finlay and Richard Ingram. London: Whurr Publishers, 2005, 100 pp., $50.00.

T. Downey

T. Downey

132 Sachems Head Road
Guilford, CT 06437
E-mail: twdowney@comcast.net

T. Downey  

I will approach this monograph by Thomas Freeman, the late, eminent pioneering psychoanalyst of Northern Ireland, in terms of what I infer to be its evoked ambience. Motherland of Psychoanalysis is a work with a vague history. An editors' note indicates that he was working on one section of it at the time of his death, but when was the rest produced? The monograph itself is bracketed with tributes to Freeman that yield a snapshot of the magnetic personage who could give life to his written word in a way that seems sadly lacking on the printed page alone. His followers introduce readers to enough of his humanity, tenacity, modesty, and dedication to understanding mental life in its most far-reaching contexts for us to begin to grasp why he was so seminal to psychiatry and psychoanalysis in Northern Ireland. It is somewhat paradoxical that the loving characterizations of Freeman by Lord Alderdice in his preface, and by Jeremy Holmes in his introduction, struck me as more vivid, fresh, and informative than much of Freeman's subsequent text. Freeman is the medium for his message. The power of his words, written and spoken, was enhanced by the power of his person during his lifetime. Historically, for me, his most momentous accomplishment was his role as founder of the Northern Ireland Association for the Study of Psychoanalysis, an effort made all the more notable because it occurred, in large part, in the midst of a civil war. Those fortunate enough to have met Tom Freeman will undoubtedly recall him as a man of courage, quiet strength, gentle wit, great compassion, and deep convictions about life and his clinical mission.

662

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: mshulmanphd@gmail.com

Michael Shulman  

I keep trying to understand Lacan, by reading him and those who have tried to explicate his ideas, by involving myself in a Lacan study group,

1043

and by talking with Lacanians whenever possible. But the fruits of my labors have been limited, for several reasons I've been able to identify. Lacan was not only a difficult thinker but one committed to being obscurantist. Often his reader is not sure that Lacan wanted to be understood. There is almost always something “barbed” (as if intentionally covered in barbed wire, as if he wanted to cause pain and to antagonize in the encounter) about the way that Lacan writes. Also, my inherited Anglo-American philosophical categories do not seem to serve as a basis to support the encounter with Lacan. Finally, I'm not a reader of French, although I also suspect that being a French reader would be of relatively little help in grasping Lacan's thought. Collectively, these issues pose a formidable barrier, if not a barricade, to my understanding Lacan.

I am no stranger to challenging texts. But there is no writer harder to read than Lacan. He is more challenging than Heidegger or Hegel. It is difficult to think that a country that has given us the prose of Montaigne, Rousseau, Voltaire, Flaubert, Sartre, and Levi-Strauss would also have produced, and supported, Lacan's. In his most famous published papers, at least, one often has the impression that he had mastered a form of thinking and speaking that is akin to automatic writing, or a nonpsychotic form of schizophasia. One often feels the more pained for having attempted the encounter with him, as in a meeting with the thorniest rosebush.

I have come to believe that persistence pays certain dividends though. At its best, Lacanian thought opens new vistas on the psychoanalytic discovery. Reading Lacan helps sharpen awareness of the ultimate power, and ultimate ingenuity, of the unconscious. Lacan highlights the Faustian elements in Freud's first inventions of psychoanalysis better than anyone else I've read. He is also helpful in formulating the nature, and paradoxes, of both the objects and the aims of human wishes, filling a major gap in Anglo-American psychoanalysis. Finally, his reading allows contemplation of a broader question of great interest as psychoanalysis develops: To what points, how far afield, can one travel in psychoanalytic thinking, starting out from a common “base camp” in Freud? (Reading Klein and Winnicott raises the same question.) What is a permissible limit of psychoanalytic thinking?

[[RunningHead]]

Response to Austin

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Via dei Fienaroli 36
00153 Rome
ITALY
E-mail: dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it

Riccardo Lombardi  

The world is deep,

Deeper than day had been aware.

—Nietszche / Mahler (Third Symphony)

I am very pleased by the interest JAPA has shown in my article and feel honored by the response John Austin has generously composed, enriching us with the creativity and accomplished musicianship that inform his point of view.

He rightly stresses the specificity of the use of music in treatment, which “can occur only in instances dictated by the particular sensibilities of analyst, patient, or both” (p. 1218). I find particularly valuable the interest in the specificity of experience shown by a group of analysts and analytic couples, putting into perspective a certain positivist absolutism in psychoanalytic thought. Thus, as Loewald (1974) astutely observed, “we begin to recognize that science and art are not as far apart from one another as Freud and his scientific age liked to assume. Science's dignity is not readily offended today by the suggestion that both art and science make use of creative imagination” (p. 352).

“Obviously, it is not necessary for an analyst to be a music lover capable of revelatory musical associations,” Austin further states (p. 1217). As I suggest in my paper, the musical perspective on time and reverie seems to present broader possibilities if it is viewed more inclusively as a model of exchange at a protosensory level that can unfold in keeping with the characteristics of our different sensibilities and various specialized sense organs. It is no accident that Austin perceives my music-related experiences as “a sort of psychic body language helpful in creating an ambience that invites the patient's explorations” (p. 1217). In emphasizing the activation of this “sort of psychic body language,” he seems to underline its placement in the more general sphere of mind-body relations. I stress this aspect because the musical melodies I refer to in my article evoke the sensory melodies (visual, kinesthetic, gustatory, etc.) known to every

Translated by Karen Christenfeld.

responseSet.title: The Ecological Dimension of Psychoanalysis and the Concept of Inner Sustainability
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ecological Dimension of Psychoanalysis and the Concept of Inner Sustainability

Wolfgang Ley

Wolfgang Ley

Werderstrasse 23
69120 Heidelberg
GERMANY
E-mail: leyheidelberg@aol.com

Wolfgang Ley  

We shall never completely subdue nature; our body, too, is an organism, itself a part of nature, and will always contain the seeds of dissolution, with its limited powers of adaptation and achievement.

—Sigmund Freud (1930)

The conditions of the possibility of experience are at the same time the conditions of the possibility of the objects of experience.

—Immanuel Kant (1781)

An “ecological-cum-psychoanalytic” perspective elucidates the innate kinship between modern, critical ecological thinking and the assumptions on the nature of the human animal underlying Freudian psychoanalysis. “Critical ecology” engages with the issues posed by a meaningful, “sustainable” design for the relationship between nature and culture; psychoanalysis investigates and engages therapeutically with human self-relations in the field of tension existing between the culture-imprinted and culture-productive “ego,” on the one hand, and the independent, naturally established motivational sides of the psyche subsumed by Freud under the term “id” on the other. Against an ecological-cum-psychoanalytic backdrop, modern developments in object relations theory and self psychology can be understood in a way that places them in a conceptual framework corresponding to Freud's central concern with the balance or integration—successful or unsuccessful—of the motivational (interactional) strivings of “internal nature” and the requirements posed by human “self-production” via culture. Psychoanalysis and critical ecology, it is argued, stand to profit from one another.

[[RunningHead]]

Appreciations

Killing in the Name of Identity. By Vamik Volkan. Charlottesville, VA: Pitchstone Publishing, 2006, 307 pp., $29.95.

Marvin Margolis

Marvin Margolis

31425 Baffin Drive
Franklin, Michigan 48025
E-mail: mmargolis@comcast.net

Marvin Margolis  

In the post-9/11 world, we are justifiably preoccupied with the threat to our survival posed by ethnic and religious hatred. Can the insights of psychoanalysis offer any hope that the dark forces of destruction can be somewhat contained and ameliorated? In this moving book, Vamik Volkan provides a cautiously hopeful message of the potential of psychoanalytic understanding to effect positive change in the troubled lives of groups caught up in seemingly intractable violence and massive trauma. Volkan is arguably the leading authority on the application of psychoanalytic theory to the wide range of international conflicts he has staked out for intervention.

Volkan's thirty years of unique contributions powerfully demonstrates how one psychoanalyst and a group of collaborators have significantly contributed to resolving group tensions adversely impacting the lives of thousands around the world. In 1987 he established the Center for the Study of the Mind and Human Interaction (CSMHI) at the medical school of the University of Virginia in Charlottesville. It became the base for his years of international efforts.

This volume contains many examples of Volkan's experiences in the international arena, as well as a presentation of the innovative concepts he has applied to group data unfamiliar to the average clinician. It is only after conflicts that have led to violence, terror, and war have been successfully terminated by military force that we see the long-lasting sequelae of massive trauma and the possibilities of mediation through intervention. Volkan is at times stunning in his understanding of global conflicts; frequently he provides clinical insights the most experienced clinical analyst could learn from. I hope that my selections from this multifaceted work will do justice to its richness.

The book's first section deals with Volkan's experiences in Georgia, a nation located between Russia and Turkey.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (Vol. 62). Edited by Robert A. King, Peter B. Neubauer, Samuel Abrams, and A. Scott Dowling. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2007, 384 pp., $65.00.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: tshapiro@med.cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro  

This volume of Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, the sixty-second, celebrates the one hundred fiftieth anniversary of the birth of Sigmund Freud. Like its predecessors, the volume is filled with scholarly and clinically arresting contributions from psychoanalysts across the nation and from the U.K. Its opening essay, a preface by Samuel Abrams, offers a detailed theoretical review of the developmental principles that have been central to psychoanalytic theory almost from the beginning. He draws also on the rich literature of academic developmental science to show the binding wisdom that has emerged from the work of developmentalists of every stripe. He advocates a constructivist approach that reaches forward from both Sigmund and Anna Freud, while at the same time he recounts the obstacles to clarity that arise from the special circumstances of the clinical perspective and the complexity of integrating data from extraclinical sources.

The larger text making up the volume pulls the reader back into history even as it reaches toward the future. A tacit theme that can be extracted from the articles presented concerns an early expansion of technique and theory followed by a period of cautious conservatism. Most recently there has been a new wave of application and expansion of psychoanalytic theory and practice to institutional work and the care of abused and indigent children, reflected in a literature with a firmer developmental base.

The volume features a special section on the Little Hans case, first published in 1909, that reconsiders the landmark analysis of a young child by Freud through the boy's father. The case was Freud's first opportunity to explore and verify his view that there is no adult neurosis without a childhood neurosis and to witness symptom formation during childhood in statu nascendi using his ideas about the centrality of the nuclear conflict, the oedipal constellation. This case, well known and often reconsidered, is now reexamined in light of new information gathered from the Freud Archives and subsequently available information about the well-known adult and public figure, Herbert Graf, who emerged from behind the screen of Freud's anonymous case history.

[[RunningHead]]

The Action in Therapeutic Action: Nonverbal Interventions

Henry Evans

Henry Evans

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1411A
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: hmemdsc@gmail.com

Henry Evans  

The panel presented varying perspectives on the role and nature of nonverbal interventions in therapeutic action. After the panelists gave their varying views on the topic, open discussion was conducted with the audience in a small-group format, with panelists and other facilitators active in each group. The formal presentations will be summarized here.

Chair Judith Chused described an evolution in psychoanalytic thinking about therapeutic action. Psychoanalysis began as “the talking cure,” with verbal interventions getting the most attention, both by practitioners and in analytic training. Remembering was contrasted with repeating, and the assumed goal for the analyst was to facilitate the patient's cognitive integration of what was being experienced and to convert repetitions into remembrances. The analyst was to avoid participating in interactions being sought by the patient (these were assumed to be unconsciously motivated re-creations), and instead was to put into words whatever the patient sought to re-create. Countertransferences were seen as an impediment to analytic work, as problems for which the analyst was to seek analytic help.

More recently, countertransferences and enactments have been generally acknowledged to occur in every deeply felt analysis, their absence being understood as a signal to the analyst that true engagement with the patient is not occurring (though many analysts believe that exploration and articulation in words is necessary if enactments are to be therapeutically useful). It has become evident that many implicit communications occur between analyst and patient and have therapeutic effect, often without ever being explicitly verbalized. For example, analysts' willingness to “wear” the attributes of a patient's transference perception, acceptance of

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Access to Psychoanalytic Ideas in American Undergraduate Institutions

Jonathan Redmond PHD and Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

13101 Allen Road, Suite 100
Southgate, Ml 48195
E-mail: jon_redo@yahoo.com

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, Ml 48104
E-mail: mshulmanphd@gmail.com

Michael Shulman   Associate faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; adjunct faculty, University of Michigan, Madonna University, and University of Toledo.

To determine the prevalence of teaching about psychoanalytic ideas in the undergraduate curricula of 150 highly ranked colleges and universities, a software-based search was conducted to find references to psychoanalytic content in published course catalogues. Results showed that psychoanalytic ideas were represented somewhere in the curricula of most (though not all) of these schools, and that overall there were many times more courses featuring psychoanalytic ideas outside psychology departments than within them. The data also suggest that there are regional differences in the likelihood an undergraduate will encounter psychoanalytic ideas at these schools. Though psychoanalytic ideas are available in some form in most of these schools' psychology departments, the average number of courses per school is small. At the same time, psychoanalytic ideas have found applications in many areas of the humanities and social sciences. The nature of the presentation of psychoanalytic ideas in these areas, however, may often be unfamiliar to clinically oriented analysts, as seen in examples of the courses that were found. Challenges and opportunities of the current academic climate vis-à-vis organized psychoanalysis are described and various suggestions made regarding how analysts can engage the academic world to its benefit.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic and Musical Perspectives on Shame in Donizetti's Lucia Di Lammermoor

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

400 Maynard Street, Suite 706
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel   Associate Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Clinical Instructor, University of Michigan Department of Psychiatry.

Two disciplines, psychoanalysis and music, are synthesized here with an eye to the origins and vicissitudes of shame and guilt as seen in the emotional disintegration of the eponymous heroine of Donizetti's opera Lucia di Lammermoor. Lucia's affects and her intrapsychic and interpersonal dynamics are heard in the music itself. A psychoanalytic and musical analysis of the opera, taking Lucia's dynamics as a quasi-substitute for clinical material, illuminates the intersections between certain theoretical aspects of the two disciplines. Both manifest and latent themes are expressed through the music of Donizetti's score.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic History

Edith Jacobson: Sie selbst und die Welt ihrer Objekte, Leben, Werk, Erinnerungen. Edited by Ulrike May and Elke Muehlleitner. Bibliothek der Psychoanalyse. Giessen: Psychosozial Verlag, 2005, 447 pp.

Rachel Baker

Rachel Baker

Rachel Baker  

This interesting volume about Edith Jacobson is available to the German-speaking public. Its subtitle translates thus: “She herself and the world of her objects, life, work, and reminiscences.” A collection of essays about Jacobson and her thinking by several contributors, it also includes autobiographical and theoretical writings by Jacobson herself. The book is divided into three parts: Edith Jacobssohn in Germany; Between the Continents; and Edith Jacobson in America (note the changed spelling of her name). One needs to read the whole book to obtain a coherent image of Jacobson's persona and the importance of her work.

Known to us in North America primarily for such works as The Self and the Object World (1964), integrating object relations and structural theory, and Depression: Comparative Studies of Normal, Neurotic, and Psychotic Conditions (1971), Edith Jacobson is presented in these pages multidimensionally. She emerges as a courageous, creative, independent thinker, in many ways far ahead of her time. She was a declared feminist who felt strongly that women should have careers. Early in her work she was looking for the common ground of the various psychoanalytic and psychological groups and appreciated the focus of ego psychology. She led the way not only in integrating biology and psychoanalytic thinking, thereby demonstrating the relevance of psychoanalytic thinking to severe pathology, but also in quietly integrating Kleinian thinking with ego psychology.

The clinically based child psychoanalytic writings of her Berlin years (considered in Part I) established her as one of the first great child analysts, positioned in her thinking between Anna Freud and Melanie Klein.

Editors' note: Dr. Ruth Baker died suddenly while engaged in the final revisions of this review. We appreciated her enthusiasm, openness, efficiency, and intelligence and deeply regret her loss.

305

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Curious Intimacy: Art and Neuro-Psychoanalysis. By Lois Oppenheim. New York: Routledge, 2005, 208 pp., $52.95.

Lee Brauer and Rima Brauer

Rima Brauer

65 Memorial Drive, Suite 440
West Hartford, CT 06107
E-mail: lbrauer@attglobal.net
E-mail: rimalb@attglobal.net

Rima Brauer  

Lois Oppenheim has written an ambitious book that poses several challenges and advances several theses. She states that “the primary impulse of creativity is homeostatic inasmuch as creativity serves to augment self-awareness and it is on awareness of self that homeostasis depends” (p. 6). She broadens the term homeostasis beyond its application to the regulation of blood and tissue fluids to include psychic homeostasis, cultural or social homeostasis, and, most generally, somatopsychic equilibration. Oppenheim argues that “there is a discharge of bodily drive in representation—neuropsychological function is significant (in more than the obvious ways) for both the making and reception of art” (p. 5). She argues that homeostasis is a linchpin in aesthetic theory. As the psychoanalytic model of the mind has broadened to encompass neuroscientific data, she argues that aesthetic theory broadens to integrate the self-awareness of the artist as embedded in a homeostatic matrix. Our idea of aesthetic theory encompasses the spectator who perceives the work of the artist. It strikes us that Oppenheim does not distinguish between art and artist. She assumes that if the artist generates something, then the production is art. Three examples in the second part of the book illustrate her thesis.

We thought the word curious in her title itself wonderful because it allows readers the full range of their imagination. We imagine that the artist is always experimental and exploratory in attitude, curious to see what ensues, so takes the next brushstroke, writes the next line of dialogue, or plays the next chord progression. Sometimes curiosity is expressed with awareness and memory and sometimes through a drunk or depressive episode. Perhaps, as with Klee's scleroderma, a fatal disease that causes the skin and connective tissue to tighten, curiosity expresses a struggle with the progressive change in form and function. Oppenheim uses curious to mean relevant so that she can argue the connection between art and neuropsychoanalysis, rather than amplifying curiosity as a motivating factor in making art. Although the title of the book implies a relationship between art and psychoanalysis, we think the relationship is between the artist and psychoanalysis.

responseSet.authorCitation: Vanheule, S., Desmet, M. & Meganck, R.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dependent and Self-Critical Depression: Evidence for Subtypes?

Stijn Vanheule , Mattias Desmet and Reitske Meganck

Reitske Meganck

H. Dunantlan 2
B-9000 Ghent
BELGIUM
E-mail: stijn.vanheule@ugent.be

Reitske Meganck  

In psychiatric and clinical psychological literature much attention has been paid to subtyping the heterogeneous group of depressed patients. Perhaps best known are the dependent or sociotropic and the self-critical or autonomous subtypes of depression proposed by Sidney Blatt and Aaron Beck, derived from psychoanalytic and cognitive-behavioral theory. This poster concentrates on Blatt's theory.

From the 1970s on, Blatt formulated a theory that distinguishes the two subtypes (for an overview, see Blatt 1998, 2004). Dependent depressed patients are characterized by preoccupation with interpersonal issues such as trust, caring, intimacy, and sexuality; their depression is frequently triggered by object loss, and they often make suicidal gestures. Self-critical depressed patients are primarily preoccupied with establishing and maintaining a sense of self, issues of autonomy and control, and other- or self-directed aggression; the latter form of aggression is obvious in their focus on self-worth and failure and in their frequent serious suicide attempts. As Blatt (1998) notes, “Introjective patients are more concerned with establishing, protecting and maintaining a viable self-concept than they are about the quality of their interpersonal relations and achieving feelings of trust, warmth and affection” (p. 731). The strengths of this theory are its clinical roots (i.e., psychoanalytic case studies that illustrate the clinical relevance of distinguishing the two subtypes) and the fact that the theory has engendered a line of empirical research. This research provides evidence that documents the validity of the distinction, and has led to insights into depression that are of interest to scholars and clinicians outside of psychoanalysis. The instrument Blatt developed to assess dependency and self-criticism is the Depressive Experiences Questionnaire (DEQ). His theory has also elicited criticisms, one of which we address in this poster—the assertion that Blatt's categorical distinction between subtypes of depression is arbitrary (see Coyne and Whiffen 1995, pp. 360-361).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Iconic, Indexical, and Symbolic Understanding: Commentary on Aragno

Donald Favareau

Donald Favareau

BLK ADM, Level 6
10 Kent Ridge Crescent
Singapore 119260
E-mail: favareau@biosemiotics.org

Donald Favareau   Faculty, University Scholars Programme, National University of Singapore.

As a member of a small but growing group of interdisciplinarians dedicated to the study of sign processes as an multilevel phenomenon integral to the organization of both human and animal life, I was greatly pleased to discover that a similar line of thinking is now being championed within the psychoanalytic discourse, at least as presented by Anna Aragno. Her trenchant and astute article, “The Language of Empathy,” reflects the ideas of those undertaking situated analyses of human meaning-making as it emerges simultaneously from both linguistic and sublinguistic planes of interaction.

I should state at the outset, however, that as a “biosemiotician” whose disciplinary training was first in philosophy of mind and later the neurobiology of language, I am professionally unequipped to comment on Aragno's discussion of the role of negotiating nonlinguistically mediated meaning within the profession-specific context of psychoanalytic clinical practice. Therefore, as to the merits of her specific claims regarding the proper measure of psychical “distance” between analyst and analysand, or to the discussion of how best the practicing psychoanalyst, while in session, should go about negotiating, on a necessarily moment-to-moment basis, “the full continuum of experience—from organic sensations, representational images, and raw emotions, to verbal expressions, metaphors, enactments, dynamics—that constitute the vast range of unconscious meanings that are verbalized and interpreted in the clinical situation” (p. 715), my own expertise can add nothing other than the commonplace, if still often undervalued, observation that such multimodal communicative situations are ones that all participants to human interaction find themselves occupied in co-constructing and negotiating with one another, always.

783

[[RunningHead]]

Empathy and Identification in Von Donnersmarck's The Lives of Others

Diana Diamond

Diana Diamond

50 Riverside Drive, Apt. 6A
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: dianadiam@aol.com

Diana Diamond   Associate Professor of Psychology, City College and the Graduate and University Center of the City of New York; Adjunct Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; candidate, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Florian Henckel von Donnersmarck's The Lives of Others, set in the German Democratic Republic in 1984, five years before the fall of the Berlin Wall, has been called the first accurate depiction of the psychological terror wielded by the Stasi, the East German secret police, who safeguarded the dictatorship of the proletariat. The film is about the psychological and political transformation of a Stasi officer, Wiesler, who undertakes the surveillance of a prominent playwright and his actress lover. The mechanisms through which Wiesler comes to empathize and identify with the subjects of his investigation, as he observes and listens in on the rich blend of passion, poetry, and politics that characterizes their lives, are explored in depth. Wiesler's transformation is based in part on the capacity to form implicit models of the behavior and experiences of others, based on the mirror neuron system, that Gallese and his colleagues call “embodied simulation.” Underpinning the processes of empathy and identification so central to this film, embodied simulation is an unconscious and prereflexive mechanism through which the actions, emotions, and sensations we observe activate internal representations of the bodily and mental states of the other. Embodied simulation also expands our understanding of the power of the primal scene, which has long been identified as a major organizer of unconscious fantasies and conflicts throughout life, and which forms the central metaphor of the film. Embodied simulation scaffolds our aesthetic response to art, music, and literature, underlies the dynamics of spectatorship, and potentially catalyzes resistance to totalitarian mass movements.

[[RunningHead]]

Out of the Woods: Tales of Resilient Teens. By Stuart T. Hauser, Joseph P. Allen, and Eve Golden. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2006, 321 pp., $27.95.

Henri Parens

Henri Parens

111 Presidential Boulevard, Suite 133
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
E-mail: hparens@verizon.net

Henri Parens  

A highly useful psychoanalytically informed contribution to our knowledge of psychic recovery and strength, this book reports on a creatively conceived psychodynamic research study and humanely conceived treatment of seriously compromised and traumatized children who went on to become very troubled and very difficult youngsters. It is especially praiseworthy for its aims and innovative strategies in presenting an insufficiently researched domain of prevention against illness and of healing from it—that is, on psychodynamic aspects of resilience in the face of harshly traumatizing early life experience.

The study of resilience began some fifty years ago. Its champions, however, have been surprisingly few, and it has been surprisingly marginalized in mental health disciplines, especially so in psychoanalysis. Individual clinicians have recognized that unique attributes contribute to “making it in life” or not making it, but have ascribed them to rather undefined capabilities or failures (e.g., Leon J. Saul [1947], Margaret S. Mahler, Erik Erikson, Robert Coles) and for a period some (e.g., E. James Anthony [1974]) have even spoken provocatively of children who are “invulnerable” (Michael Rutter [2000]).

325

[[RunningHead]]

Endings and Beginnings: On Terminating Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. By Herbert J. Schlesinger. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2005, 256 pp., $59.95.

Richard Munich

Richard Munich

286 Madison Avenue, PH 23
New York, NY 10017
E-mail: armunich@msn.com

Richard Munich  

The idea that the ending of psychotherapeutic or psychoanalytic treatment is not a fixed matter but more a function of its beginning, its process, and the unique relationship between each patient and therapist is the foundation of

654

this wise and compactly written sequel to the author's book on technique (Schlesinger 2003). Like that first book, to which the author makes abundant reference and wherein this project was promised, Beginnings and Endings is a compendium of common and uncommon sense. As if one were sitting at a master's feet, this sense begins with Schlesinger's oft-stated idea that ending is what therapy and analysis are all about. Something very close to this theme is taken up and echoed in Joseph Caston's recent contribution in this journal, in which he argues that the narrative structures inherent in both poems and treatments prefigure their endings (Caston 2007). This idea of intertwined endings and beginnings leads the reader beyond mere matters of technique, such that the book stands as an important contribution to our field's ongoing discussion of therapeutic action.

Already featured in a lively and well-attended Meet-the-Author discussion at the 2007 winter meeting of APsaA, the book opens with a no-frills preface for the “browser,” in which the author provides us the gist of his argument. This overview permits the reader to fully engage with what follows, as chapters range from definitions to patterns (with illustrations) and indicators for ending treatment, to working on termination with vulnerable patients, the problem of impasse or stalemate, the vicissitudes of loss and mourning, therapist burnout, and other countertransference phenomena. As in the earlier book, the author suggests explicit, jargon-free, patient-centered interventions that engage the patient's latent and often guarded thoughts about termination while at the same time moving matters forward. It is a bonus that the life and mind of the therapist/analyst figure prominently but without intrusiveness throughout the book.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Academic Exchange on Empathy

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

In creating his new scientific psychology, Freud often used the vocabulary of the vernacular German of his time. In doing so, he forever changed the meaning of many common words, such as Verneinung (negation), setting in motion a process of conceptual change as subsequent psychoanalytic writers continue to use his terms in new contexts. As changes accrue, periodic review of a specific concept can provide us an overview of its history of usage and can highlight crucial distinctions. In addition, we may be moved to reexamine key concepts by the emergence of new knowledge from neighboring disciplines and to reevaluate the relevance of such knowledge to our clinical and theoretical concerns.

The topic of this academic exchange—Einfühlung (empathy)—is a term that over the course of Freud's work—and especially after Freud through the writings of Heinz Kohut (1959)—has evolved from its everyday use to become a psychoanalytic concept of increasing importance. The two psychoanalytic papers that form the core of this section demonstrate that empathy has emerged as a key concept for our field, worthy of critical examination. Anna Aragno argues for differentiating analytic empathic listening from the ordinary quotidian variety; the latter is the hallmark of all our intersubjective experiences, whose sources she locates in the earliest caretaker-child exchanges. For their part, Siegfried Zepf and Sebastian Hartmann argue for distinctions within the day-to-day working world of analytic practice; specifically, they explore the relation of empathy to analysts' countertransference reactions to their patientsanalysts' transferences.

Although the goal of both papers is a better understanding of empathy in its specifically psychoanalytic sense, the authors cite research findings from other fields. They thus acknowledge the broader scope of the concept of empathy as it has been addressed both by the neurosciences following the discovery of the mirror neuron system and by infant-caretaker observational research on affect attunement and early communicational exchanges.

[[RunningHead]]

Response to Favareau and Gallese

Anna Aragno

Anna Aragno

140–142 West End Avenue, #23C
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Annaragno@earthlink.net

Anna Aragno  

In expressing my appreciation to Vittorio Gallese and Donald Favareau for their commentaries, and particularly to Bonnie Litowitz for generating this dialogue, I have only the following brief thoughts: Since its inception, psychoanalysis has been an epistemology that crosses borderlines of mind, brain, and related disciplines, with a polyperspectival interpretive spread and an interdisciplinary vista. In an age of overspecialization, when disciplines fragment knowledge, generating their own vocabularies and frames of reference, genuine interdisciplinarity is an even harder ideal to reach—yet reach it and maintain it we must. The expansive biosemiotic framework elaborated by Favareau, together with Gallese's hardcore neuroscientific research, not only broadens our database regarding empathic processes but provides the perfect marriage between the study of “mind” and the study of “brain,” both of which must enter into the psychoanalytic reservoir of new knowledge. If psychoanalysis is to remain the ideal methodology for the study of conscious and unconscious processes, its metatheoretical foundations must evolve and grow into a genuine science of mind. To achieve this, we must continue to be enriched by, and aim for, maximum cross-fertilization and a sophisticated integration of knowledge from adjacent fields, as exemplified by this cross-disciplinary discussion. Today the dialogue focuses on Einfühlung; tomorrow on another topic. In my opinion, this is exactly the sort of interactive dialogue that our field will most benefit by.

803

[[RunningHead]]

In Memoriam: Stuart Hauser

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

It is with great sadness that I and everyone at JAPA mourn the loss of Stuart Hauser, our long-serving Associate Editor for Research, who died unexpectedly on August 5, 2008, of complications related to his treatmnt for esophageal cancer. JAPA, the psychoanalytic community, his beloved Harvard, the many other institutions and organizations to which he belonged, and of course his family and friends share in our collective grief. It is fitting that this issue contains one of his many contributions to our literature, part of a legacy of research and scholarship that characterized his illustrious professional career. At JAPA, Stuart catalyzed an ongoing commitment to bringing the best empirical research relative to psychoanalysis to our readers, always demanding clear, concise, and accessible expression in order to present research to a primarily clinical readership. The many authors who worked with Stuart can attest to his insistent demand for excellence and his tireless availability for help in improving manuscripts. Stuart was deeply committed to enhancing the status of psychoanalysis in the empirical science community, a commitment shared by all of us at JAPA. His own research over a long and productive career, the interest and generosity he showed to younger colleagues, his courtly grace, and his good humor endeared Stuart to his many collaborators and students worldwide. We at JAPA will try to do justice to his scholarly legacy. He will be missed.

1079

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Academic Exchange: Time, Music, and Reverie

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Music has always been of interest to psychoanalysts, who recognize that it affords a powerful form for affective expression. We listen for the music in our patients' speech, the prosody that indicates the significance, and often the true meaning, of their spoken words. In this way we listen like the baby listens to its mother's voice, as she listens to the baby, before words have meanings.

In this academic exchange, Riccardo Lombardi calls our attention to another dimension of listening to music: music evoked in an analyst's reverie as he listens to his patients struggle to express and contain their unformed and overwhelming emotions. Lombardi describes patients who failed to receive needed, early dyadic support for affect regulation and how music, intruding in the analyst's reverie enabled a reparative attunement to be reengaged in the analytic couple. In trying to understand what feature of music might be critical for the expression and containment of affects, Lombardi focuses on the importance of time.

To deepen our understanding of the interrelations of time, music, and emotion, we asked John Austin, a composer, to provide his thoughts on the themes in Lombardi's paper. He could not, of course, comment on the analyst's reverie, but he has graciously provided us a rare glimpse into the perspective of an analysand for whom music is equally evocative.

John Austin studied composition while a Harvard undergraduate and later in Vienna and Chicago, receiving his doctorate in music from the University of Chicago in 1981. From 1981 to 1999 he supported his composing by practicing law (having graduated from Harvard Law School in 1960); he now devotes his time fully to music. His works have been performed in Chicago and Cambridge (Massachusetts), and at the Tanglewood, Aspen, and Door County Music Festivals. He is currently collaborating on an opera with his wife, Christine Froula, a Northwestern University professor of English.

[[RunningHead]]

Prediction of Improvement in Medical Treatment for Depression: Using Self-Report Measures of Erikson's Stages

Marcia Kaplan , Karl Stukenberg and Megan Kruty

Megan Kruty

3001 Highland Avenue Suite A
Cincinnati, OH 45219
E-mail: mkaplan@3001.us

Xavier University

Megan Kruty  

Patients with depression are known to vary widely in their response to treatment with antidepressant medication (Hirschfeld and Goodwin 1988; Spigset and Martensson 1999). The primary variable that predicts response to treatment is the level of the individual's psychological functioning at the beginning of treatment (Bifulco et al. 1998). In this study we tested the

1348

hypothesis that patients' ratings of their developmental fixations would predict treatment response over and above initial evaluations of depressive symptomatology. Earlier research has shown that patients with treatment-resistant depression often have early childhood trauma or other life stressors that may contribute to the difficulty in achieving remission of depressive symptoms (Dube et al. 2005; Heim and Nemeroff 2001). Early childhood trauma and/or ongoing developmental obstacles beyond infancy could lead to serious obstacles to satisfaction in adult life. External events that upset tenuous balances (e.g., job loss, divorce, children leaving for college, retirement of a spouse) can unmask problems in self-regulation that culminate in major depression (Spigset and Martensson 1999); depressions of this sort are not easily resolved with medication treatment (Nemeroff et al. 2003). The authors sought a means of identifying depressed patients' baseline psychological functioning apart from their symptom presentation and correlating this baseline with response to treatment.

Method

Patients were recruited by the lead author from her practice at a large university mental health center. All subjects gave written consent according to Institutional Review Board protocol for the university and underwent testing with the Structured Clinical Interview for Depression (SCID), the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), and the Inventory for Psychosocial Balance (IPB) described below.

Each patient was evaluated clinically and assigned a Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF) score by the treating psychiatrist. At follow-up twelve months after enrollment, each patient repeated the self-report IPB and BDI, and the GAF was again scored by the treating psychiatrist, who was blind to IPB scores. Response to antidepressant medication, which was prescribed according to standard algorithm (antidepressants were used at the highest tolerable dose, with a second antidepressant added if necessary, and augmentation as indicated with lithium, second-generation antipsychotics, and/or stimulants) by an experienced psychopharmacologist, was assessed at follow-up by change in the BDI and GAF scores.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Art

John Sloan's Women: A Psychoanalysis of Vision. By Janice M. Coco. Newark: University of Delaware Press, 2004, 135 pp., $45.00.

Donald Kuspit

Donald Kuspit

38 West 26th Street
New York, NY 10010-2012
E-mail: kuspitn@aol.com

Donald Kuspit  

Today John Sloan (1871–1951) would be regarded as a conservative artist. Once he was regarded as a revolutionary, along with Robert Henri, his mentor, and other members of The Eight, later expanded into the Ashcan School. So-called “artist-reporters,” their work was discredited as “vulgar” by academic traditionalists, particularly those affiliated with New York's influential National Academy of Design. Responding to the urban environment, particularly the “low life” in immigrant neighborhoods, their work was too realistic, crudely executed, and emotionally provocative for the establishment, which demanded polished images of polite people. It was a familiar battle, fought many times in the modern era, between the hoi polloi and the upper bourgeoisie, in America between a Whitman-inspired democratic “street art” and the nouveau riche idea of art's “highness,” making it suitable for conspicuous consumption; between a suppressive idea of safe art and the view of art as irrepressible expression informed by personal experience; more generally, between respectable art that obeys long-sanctioned rules and the reckless impropriety of a “lawless” art. Sloan, with his voyeuristic view of backyard events—intimate events in tenement apartments with curtainless windows, making it easy to spy on them with binoculars, as Sloan did—once represented the so-called “shock of the new,” in both style and theme. His once unconventional work was hard to sell, but once it became conventional and did, European modernism caught up with it (by way of the 1913 Armory Show in New York), nullifying its artistic and commercial value and reducing Sloan to a minor artist working in a retardataire, not to say obsolete, manner.

Janice Coco's first-rate book is a convincing attempt to counteract this trivializing view. She shows, with the aid of psychoanalytic ideas—mostly Freudian, with some lip service to Winnicott's idea of “potential space”—that Sloan's art is much more subtle and complex than it has been given credit for. She shows that its personal and social import correlate.

Psychanalyse des limites. By Didier Anzieu. Paris: Dunod, 2007, 312 pp., €26.00.

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139-2211
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner  

Didier Anzieu (1923-1999), a highly esteemed and productive French psychoanalyst, is perhaps best known to English-speaking analysts

1403

through his study of Freud's self-analysis (1959) and his monumental work The Skin Ego(1985). He is the author of numerous books and articles, including those related to his research in groups and psychodrama, studies of creativity, the analysis of Beckett with Bion, and explorations of neighboring fields of inquiry such as attachment, infant research, and communications theory. For this collection, his colleague Catherine Chabert has gathered sixteen of his papers, extending from 1974 to 1999, and contributed an informative introduction. She has elsewhere published a book summarizing Anzieu's career (Chabert 1997).

Anzieu's work carries the mark of a passionate quest that reflects a dual imperative to discover and to heal, commented on after his death by his student and colleague, René Käes, and his peer at the École Normale Superieur, Jean Laplanche.1 In conversations with Gilbert Tarrab (Anzieu 1986), he stated that he had become an analyst to care for his mother, a severely ill woman who in 1931 became a patient of a young Jacques Lacan and served as the subject of Lacan's doctoral thesis in psychiatry. By a strange coincidence, Anzieu himself was referred to Lacan for analysis by his mentor, Daniel Lagache, during his graduate studies at the Sorbonne. Anzieu followed Lagache and Lacan out of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society to the new French Society they founded, but left Lacan when he discovered the connection to his mother, subsequently becoming a harsh critic of his former analyst. After Lacan's expulsion by the IPA, Anzieu remained in the newly formed Association Psychanalytique de France, where he became an influential member, while pursuing for many years an academic career in clinical psychology.

Anzieu shared many interests with his better known “imaginary twin,” André Green (p. 249), notably the themes of le vide (the void, emptiness) and attachment to the negative in psychic life. Both were influenced in their work by the writings of Winnicott and Bion on the origins of the self. Both adopted Bion's concepts of container and contained in their theories, with Anzieu adding a third, related notion of an actively containing person, proposed by Käes. In his discussion of the pathologies of early deficits, Anzieu offers the notion of an indifferent mother in place of Green's dead mother, whose failure of transformative activity impairs construction of an inner world.

[[RunningHead]]

Screen Memory: Its Importance to Object Relations and Transference

Richard Reichbart

Richard Reichbart

60 West Ridgewood Avenue
Ridgewood, NJ 07450
E-mail: reichbart@earthlink.net

Richard Reichbart   Faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); Training Analyst and Supervisor, Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy of New Jersey.

A screen memory of an obsessive and narcissistic man, reported early in psychoanalysis, both represented and disguised the patient's oedipal conflict, incestuous wishes, and sibling rivalry. It symbolized for him his relationship with his mother and was treated by him, in a repetitive and fetishistic manner throughout treatment, as the reason for his bitterness toward life, his sense of entitlement, his narcissism, and his distrust of women. In the transference, the memory—far from being inert—constantly played an active role in his wishes and disappointments regarding the analyst, and in his fantasied oedipal triumph over him. As the analysis progressed, and after years of treatment, the encapsulated nature of this memory began to give way to the patient's growing awareness of his oedipal wishes, the full range of his feelings toward his mother, and his sense of abandonment by her. The nature of screen memory is explored, including how it relates to a patient's personality and use of the past in general, how it may figure in the development of a person's object relations, and the decisive role it may play throughout a treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward a General Theory of Unconscious Processes in Psychoanalysis and Anesthesiology

George Mashour

George Mashour

1500 East Medical Center Drive
Ann Arbor, Ml 48109-0048
E-mail: gmashour@umich.edu

George Mashour   Director of Neuroanesthesiology and Assistant Professor of Anesthesiology and Neurosurgery, University of Michigan Medical School.

Psychoanalysis and anesthesiology appear radically different in their clinical practice, yet they share a focus of inquiry: unconscious processes. Despite this common domain, there has been no exploration of the relationship between “the unconscious” as conceived by psychoanalysts and “surgical unconsciousness” as conceived by anesthesiologists. This is likely due to the fact that general anesthesia has been assumed to be a state in which the brain is simply “turned off.” More recent neuroscientific data invalidate this assumption by demonstrating that the anesthetized brain is both cognitively dynamic and capable of implicit learning. Current perspectives on anesthetic mechanisms suggest that general anesthesia is characterized not simply by the absence of cognitive activity, but by the disintegration of cognitive activity. The cognitive unbinding paradigm of general anesthesia is discussed and its application to Wilfred Bion's theory of thinking, as well as his concept of attacks on linking, is elucidated. Based on the common structure and function of unconscious processes in psychoanalysis and anesthesiology, the foundation of a general theory is established.

[[RunningHead]]

Hans Loewald, Psychoanalysis, and the Project of Autonomy

Joel Whitebook

Joel Whitebook

1225 Park Avenue Suite 1A
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: whitebookj@aol.com

Joel Whitebook  

For some time psychoanalysts have tended to view Freud's cultural writings—concerning modernity, secularism, science, and religion—disparagingly, seeing them as the unscientific speculations of a misguided genius. But the questions Freud explored in those works are pressing topics that deserve serious attention. Just as fascism provided the historical context in which the critical theorists of the Frankfurt School developed a psychoanalytic social theory in the 1930s and 1940s, so the rise of fundamentalism demands a similar effort today. The “project of autonomy” conceptualized by the psychoanalyst-philosopher Castoriadis can be used to situate psychoanalysis in its broader historical context, as part of the emancipatory movement of modernity, and to elucidate fundamentalism as an attempt to turn back that project and reinstate the values of premodern traditional societies. Because the widespread aversion to secularism today is in no small degree the responsibility of secularists themselves—Freud's relatively crude and simplistic disregard of some of the deepest yearnings of humankind is a case in point—it is time to formulate, using the work of Hans Loewald, a more sensitive and sophisticated psychoanalytic view of religion. Yet psychoanalytic secularists must avoid overcompensating for past mistakes by giving too much ground to antisecularists. The legitimate desire to do justice to religion must not trump the need to advance the project of autonomy as a first priority.

When Analysis Makes Patients Worse: The Negative Therapeutic Reaction Revisited

Simona Grabel

Simona Grabel

76 Bedford Street, Suite 10
Lexington, MA 02420
E-mail: mona.grabel@rcn.com

Simona Grabel  

Light though thou be, thou leapest out of darkness; but I am darkness leaping out of light, leaping out of thee!

—Herman Melville Moby Dick

Henry Smith began the session by introducing some ideas about the negative therapeutic reaction. Although it is often a puzzling phenomenon, he wondered whether it might be a reaction that appears in all analytic approaches. Do some patients respond negatively to their experience of being understood by the analyst, or to their own sense of progress, no matter the analyst's approach? Smith led us through the evolution of some of Freud's ideas about “negative reactions,” beginning with the Wolf Man. Freud's famous patient “showed a habit of producing transitory ‘negative reactions’; every time something had been conclusively cleared up, he attempted to contradict the effect for a short while by an aggravation of the symptoms which had been cleared up.” Freud reminded us that children often treat prohibitions in this same way. For example, when they are rebuked for something, they repeat it once more before stopping it. Smith noted the developmental nature of Freud's theory of the negative reaction. By 1923, Freud's more pessimistic view was evolving, as in The Ego and the Id. Freud knew that some patients react “inversely” to the treatment, demonstrating something within them that “sets itself against their recovery.” Freud identified this as a “moral” factor, “a sense of guilt, which is finding its satisfaction in the illness and refuses to give up the punishment of suffering” (p. 49). The discovery of the “unconscious sense of guilt” led Freud to study deeply masochistic patients who seemed to resist all therapeutic efforts and aims.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 20, 2007. Panelists: Henry Smith (chair), Michael Feldman, Shelley Orgel, Anna Ornstein.

[[RunningHead]]

War and Peace

Fratricide in the Holy Land: A Psychoanalytic View of the Arab Israeli Conflict. By Avner Falk. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 2004, 300 pp., $35.00.

Lord Alderdice

Lord Alderdice

Belfast BT8 8BH
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: alderdicelord@doctors.org.uk

Lord Alderdice  

In working at the review of this book, I read it through at least half a dozen times, and therein is a mixed message. Were it not a well worthwhile book I should not have spent the time on it. At the same time, some questions kept coming back to me which rereading did not quite satisfy.

I must confess that I am not entirely objective. I start from a tacit assumption that psychoanalysis has something useful to contribute to our understanding of long-running violent political conflicts, so this book already has my sympathy before I open it. The author starts out by arguing that the Arab-Israeli conflict is essentially not a rational matter. Most psychoanalysts would agree, though this is of course contrary to the view expressed by most of those who address the issue from a political, or some other psychological, point of view. His review of the literature is not very detailed, but he makes some useful points. He rightly, if rather promptly, disposes of suggestions that the roots of the problem are to be found in national characteristics or in the psychopathology of individual political leaders; he outlines the inadequacy of a rationalistic approach; and he notes the need to exercise care in applying individual psychoanalytic theory to the operation of groups. If the review chapter is relatively brief, the bibliography is enormous: at sixty pages it occupies almost a quarter of the book. Even then it does not include all the references in the text, but it is certainly a useful list for those who wish to take their reading further.

It is quickly clear that Falk feels passionately about his subject, but from the beginning he properly acknowledges that he and his fellow countrymen, like everyone else, are gripped by an emotional appreciation of “the facts” and often captured by phenomena characterized by the repetition compulsion. I found myself becoming a little uncomfortable when he called “absurd” the use of the terms Sephardic and Ashkenazi because of their psychological rather than historico-geographical accuracy.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapies in Collision: Introduction to Purcell

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

E-mail: rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

Its footfalls muffled, the psychotropic revolution has been stealing up on psychoanalytic clinical practice over the past quarter century. There is now a convincing accumulation of evidence that psychopharmacological agents are regularly deployed in conjunction with psychoanalytic treatment at all levels of experience: from training analyses of candidates to regular therapeutic analyses to the analyses performed by candidates under supervision. If we add to these all of those gray-area cases of psychoanalytic psychotherapies approaching psychoanalysis in their depth and therapeutic reach, it is obvious that the pharmaco-pervasion of our technique is significant.

Although our technique has been profoundly affected, our theories have, in contrast, lagged behind. Even as the addition of medication to psychoanalytic therapy has become a widely accepted practice, our ideas about it have remained curiously undeveloped and one-sided. Mostly we have addressed one dimension of the problem, contributing a profusion of ideas explaining the symbolism of added medication and its choreography (e.g., “split” treatments, the act of prescribing, inquiries about side effects), but we have had little to say about its “direct” effects on mind/ brain functioning. Moreover, when considering medication, analysts have accepted the diagnostic categories of the DSM and been compelled, for want of anything better, to turn to studies based on these as the only ones with sufficient statistical power to rationalize our clinical decisions. (One promise of the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual [PDM Task Force 2006] is the ultimate delineation of theoretically and empirically coherent psychodynamic categories against which various therapeutic approaches, including medication, can be tested.)

The paper that follows this introduction, by Stephen D. Purcell, defines a direction away from our exclusive reliance on DSM-based thinking. Purcell argues that even when DSM considerations suggest the use of a particular class of medications, psychoanalytic considerations may argue more strongly against such use.

responseSet.title: The Comic in Little Hans: Literary Conceit or Analytic Process? Commentary on Ahbel-Rappe
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Comic in Little Hans: Literary Conceit or Analytic Process? Commentary on Ahbel-Rappe

Silvia Bell

Silvia Bell

1801 South Road
Baltimore, MD 21209
E-mail: smvbell@aol.com

Silvia Bell   Training and Supervising Analyst and Child and Adolescent Supervising Analyst, Baltimore Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis; Training and Supervising Analyst (Geographic Rule), Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, University of Maryland School of Medicine.

To write a commentary about a literary reading of Freud's case studies invites a reconsideration of his greatness as a man of letters. Freud's works represent the confluence of literature and psychoanalysis, art and hermeneutics, that reveals and interprets the human psyche. The literary scholar Harold Bloom (1994) places Freud among those authors whose universality makes them canonical, those “authoritative in our culture” who have an inescapable influence on the writing of future generations (p. 1). Bloom underscores the role of literature as precursor to psychoanalysis. He places Shakespeare at the center of his Western canon because he “originates the depiction of self-change on the basis of self-overhearing, … [the] most remarkable of all literary innovations” (p. 46). He considers this innovation prophetic of the psychoanalytic method, in which patients achieve self-knowledge as they “overhear themselves in the context of their transference to their analysts” (p. 47). Bloom is dismissive of Freud's theoretical discoveries—he quips that “all of Freud that matters most is there in Shakespeare already” (p. 24)—yet he grants Freud's “greatness as a writer” and considers it “his actual achievement” (p. 349). Freud, avid reader of Shakespeare and ancient texts, identifies and explicates the root of conflict in desire expressed so eloquently by the poets. Freud received the Goethe prize for literature from the German government in 1930.

Literature was one of Freud's acknowledged sources, but his writing style was unique. Patrick Mahony (1987)

The author thanks Joseph Bierman for making available to her a prepublication copy of his article. Submitted for publication March 11, 2008.

[[RunningHead]]

Presumed Synergy or Antagonism in Combined Treatment: Commentary on Purcell

Fredric Busch and Larry Sandberg

Larry Sandberg

10 East 78th Street, #5A
New York, NY 10075
E-mail: fnb80@aol.com

205 East 78th Street, #1A
New York, NY 10075

Larry Sandberg   Clinical Associate Professor, Weill Cornell Medical College; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Over the last fifty years the availability of medication for the treatment of psychiatric disorders has created challenges to the theoretical framework, identity, and mode of practice of psychoanalysts (Busch 1998; Roose and Johannet 1998). During this time analysts have in general shifted from dismissal of the value of medication to viewing it as a useful adjunct to psychoanalytic treatments (Roose 1995). Purcell argues that the pendulum has now swung to an assumption of synergy and an overreliance on objective criteria that separate medication use from psychoanalytic work. He usefully warns that this attitude can lead to problematic enactments involving narcissistic and perverse resistances that disrupt the integrative efforts of an analysis.

While alerting us to a potential scotoma in our current view of psychopharmacological interventions, Purcell evinces a series of antimedication biases. Although claiming that “there are often clear medical, ethical, and legal rationales for the use of medication either as the primary treatment or as an adjunctive modality” (pp. 913–914), it is difficult to determine under what circumstances he would consider medication to be indicated in a psychoanalytic treatment. He views medication primarily as antiintegrative and disruptive of the goals of psychoanalysis. We will discuss how Purcell's stance can itself lead to problematic transference-countertransference enactments. Thus, his effort to correct the problems of presumed synergy contains important remnants of an antimedication bias that subvert his primary goals, to foster an optimal analytic attitude and process.

[[RunningHead]]

Unattainable Goals in Psychoanalysis

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

400 Maynard Street, Suite 706
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel  

Chair Owen Renik introduced the panelists and posed three open-ended questions for them to answer: (1) Freud characterized psychoanalysis as an “impossible profession.” What does that mean to you? What are the advantages and disadvantages of that conception? (2) How can it be useful for an analyst to pursue an unattainable goal? Give a brief clinical example. (3) How can it be harmful for an analyst to pursue an unattainable goal? Give a brief clinical example. After each question was “answered,” there were exchanges among the panelists and with the audience.

Question One

Steven Cooper began by stating that there is a disadvantage to considering psychoanalysis an impossible profession. He spoke about the avoidance of accepting and integrating the limitations of our patients, ourselves, and the analytic process. Cooper emphasized the complexity of the psychoanalytic intersection between the minds of patient and analyst, stating that unconscious process is always limited by what the patient says and the analysts hears, resulting in a temporary state of awareness. Referring to the interaction of the analytic dyad as the “analysis of enactment,” Cooper emphasized how enactment is ubiquitous, constantly in need of analysis privately or aloud, is always in motion, and, by definition, limits an analysis. Further, the analysis of enactment—or interpersonal engagement—varies over time and becomes as difficult as the analysis of intraspychic conflict. All of this is due to the deeply personal nature of analytic work for the analyst and to the complexities of countertransference, which is highly influenced by the patient's wishes for infinite possibility. The analyst becomes the object of disappointment. Cooper cautions the analyst that idealization of the patient and the patient's sense of extemalization of

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Narrative Analysis of Helplessness in Depression

Stijn Vanheule and Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

H. Dunantlaan 2
B-9000 Ghent
BELGIUM
E-mail: stijn.vanheule@ugent.be

Stuart Hauser   Senior Scientist. Judge Baker Children's Center; Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

The transcripts of semistructured clinical interviews with forty psychiatrically hospitalized adolescents were subjected to narrative analysis in an effort to map the logic of their explanations as they spontaneously talked about helplessness experiences, and to determine how helplessness is embedded in broader story lines. Three types of narrative composition were discerned, and are discussed by means of excerpts from the interviews. In a first and predominant type of narrative, a disturbing confrontation with another is pivotal: the other's intentions are obscure; this frightens the narrator, who does not know what to do. Helplessness arises as a direct result of not knowing how to manage the “unbearable riddle” of the other's intentions. In the second, more marginal type of narrative, helplessness is embedded in an account of emptiness and boredom. The protagonist relates enduring experiences of emptiness due to loss and the suffering consequent on it. In the third, also more marginal type of narrative, helplessness is framed in a context of failure: the protagonist adheres to strict standards, feels he or she has fallen short, and concludes that he or she is a failure. Only the first type of narrative is significantly related to the psychiatric diagnoses of mood disorder and major depression.

[[RunningHead]]

Guilt and Its Vicissitudes: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Morality. By Judith M. Hughes. New York: Routledge, 2008, 160 pp., $35.95.

Frances Lang

Frances Lang

85 Kenwood Street
Brookline, MA 02446
Email: lang.f@rcn.com

Frances Lang  

In this volume, Judith Hughes traces the development of psychoanalytic thinking about guilt from Freud's writings through those of Klein and

1408

her followers to the work of Bion, Britton, and contemporary Kleinians such as Betty Joseph. Laying the groundwork with a discussion of Freud's analysis of guilt and the origins of moral development, Hughes goes on, using clinical and theoretical writings of the Kleinians, to illuminate areas of accord among themselves and Freud, as well as areas in which, in her view, they carried forward and enlarged on Freud's account of moral development. Accounts of the 1943-1944 Controversial Discussions of the British Psycho-Analytical Society are included, illuminating, in particular, disputes regarding fantasy- and reality-thinking in infants and shifts within the thinking of Klein and her followers on this topic.

“[It is] my intention to represent the sense of guilt as the most important problem in the development of civilization …” (Freud 1930, p. 134). With the introduction of the superego, Freud established the study of moral development as an aspect of the psychoanalytic enterprise, reconceptualizing guilt as an unconscious as well as conscious phenomenon. In her introduction, Hughes lays out the themes of ambivalence, omnipotence of thoughts, and the inescapability of guilt—established by Freud as central to human moral development and later elaborated and revised by Klein. As the author observes, both Freud and Klein saw a moral sense as natural, but not native, an emergent phenomenon. Using case illustrations such as those of Little Hans and the Rat Man, Hughes elucidates the centrality, for Freud, of the oedipus complex and its dissolution in the establishment of the superego. She discusses Totem and Taboo (Freud 1912-1913), which seeks to lend historical weight to this explanation of the inevitability of guilt, with the murder and devouring of the jealous and domineering patriarch by his sons and their subsequent feelings of guilt and remorse, based on the affectionate strain of their feelings for him. To his last book, Freud maintained the historicity of this event. Thus, phylogenetically as well as ontogenetically, he has guilt proceeding from the criminal intent of parricide and incest with the mother. Moreover, despite his findings regarding the omnipotence of thoughts, Freud believed that the neurotics he treated suffered guilt not only for their impulses and emotions, but also for their deeds: “In their childhood, they had … evil impulses, pure and simple, and turned them into acts so far as the impotence of childhood allowed” (Freud 1930, p. 161).

[[RunningHead]]

The, Future of Prejudice: Psychoanalysis and the Prevention of Prejudice. Edited by Henri Parens, Afaf Mahfouz, Stuart Twemlow, and David Scharff. New York: Guilford Press, 2005, 256 pp., $70.00.

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

Jerusalem
ISRAEL
E-mail: nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg  

This book, which promises much, has six strong chapters: clearly written, they present innovative ideas or studies, advance our knowledge of the field, show an understanding of psychoanalysis, and raise additional questions to be pursued. Regarding the remaining ten chapters, I invoke the words of Martin Bergmann (2007), in his introduction to the IPA's collection of critical essays on “Mourning and Melancholia”: “These … do not read easily. … the reader has to carry a greater burden.”

Let us imagine that this manuscript had been sent to a psychoanalytic journal for a special issue. What might the journal's editor have said to the four editors of this collection?

Let us start with the strengths. These chapters include Fonagy and Higgit's application of attachment theory to the ubiquity of prejudice; Young-Bruehl's update of her ideas about prejudice studies and her framework of character types; Brenner's history of the development of anti-Semitism and its resurgence; Knowlton's observations on Mormon/non-Mormon relations; Twemlow and Sacco's field trials on diminishing prejudice in school systems; and Parens's observations about benign versus malignant prejudice in early childhood.

Fonagy and Higget begin with a classical psychoanalytic construct of prejudice: disavowal of aggression and sexuality, the need to manage the anxiety occasioned by these impulses, and the use of the defense mechanisms of displacement, projection, and projective identification. They cite Bettelheim's 1943 article on prejudice as a “precursor of acted-out aggression,” commenting that much that “seems to us current in psychoanalytic thought was already familiar to psychoanalysts between the wars” (p. 64). Ironically, neither they nor the other contributors cite the joint psychoanalytic/sociological study of prejudice in returning American G.I.'s by Bettelheim and Janowitz (1964), in which they identified four levels of prejudice in some 40 percent of returning “grunts,” the most severely prejudiced of whom had histories of child abuse. But Fonagy and Higget reconceptualize attachment insecurity in terms of prejudice: dismissive insecure attachment “reduces” the “felt importance of the unmet need”; preoccupied insecure attachment reflects “the hope of forcing a caregiving response” (pp. 70–71). In either case, insecure attachment will trigger suspicion and prejudice.
